I've done JS so I'm familiar with using dot notation to access data. I've tried to take a lot of my JS patterns to python over the years and, to my surprise, ended up regretting it.

I could imagine being thrilled about adding a feature like this to kaptan 2 years ago or so.

I don't really know if kaptan should be making an opinion on accessing nested data via dot notation, or adding and removing things in this way. Then leaving it to the user if they want to 

I think a better approach would be to make it more convenient / obvious to access the underlying [dictionary](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#dictionaries) data upon import. That makes it possible to use something like:

- https://pypi.python.org/pypi/dotmap
- https://pypi.python.org/pypi/dotted
- https://pypi.python.org/pypi/supermutes
- https://pypi.python.org/pypi/neobunch
- https://pypi.python.org/pypi/munch
- https://pypi.python.org/pypi/symboldict

The other option would be to add a class argument when importing to cast the incoming dict as a SymbolDict/Bunch/DotMap object. This would ensure equivalent behavior while not duplicating behavior other dictionary wrappers do.

That's my opinion, Agree? Disagree? Thoughts?

如果需要根据消息内容或其他属性，进行不同的响应操作，只需把 `msg` 传递给其他响应函数即可。

例如

```python

# 回复其他好友
bot.register(Friend)
def reply_others(msg):
    pass

# 回复管理员
bot.register(admin)
def reply_admin(msg):
    if msg.text in defined_orders:
        # 如果是预定义的管理命令，则执行命令
        process_order(msg)
    else:
        # 否则当做普通好友进行回复
        reply_others(msg)
```

The problem is a bug within glad. Glad has the problem when mixing the GL:core with GLES1/GLES2 it doesnt generate symbols which are removed in the GL:core profile but should be added because they are defined in the GLES API.

To make that more clear an example with the `GL_LUMINANCE` symbol:

```
    <!-- Initially defined in GL 1.0 -->
    <feature api="gl" name="GL_VERSION_1_0" number="1.0">
        <require>
            <enum name="GL_LUMINANCE"/>
        </require>
    </feature>

    <!-- GL 3.2 with a core profile removes the symbol  -->
    <feature api="gl" name="GL_VERSION_3_2" number="3.2">
        <remove profile="core" comment="Compatibility-only GL 1.1 features removed from GL 3.2">
            <enum name="GL_LUMINANCE"/>
        </remove>
    </feature>

     <!-- GLES 2.0 adds the symbol again -->
    <feature api="gles2" name="GL_ES_VERSION_2_0" number="2.0">
        <require>
            <enum name="GL_LUMINANCE"/>
        </require>
    </feature>
```

Glad gets confused (actually a badly implemented parser) and removes the `GL_LUMINANCE` symbol (generated code does not include the symbol). This is very confusing behaviour, in order to mitigate the problem for now (non trivial change to glad) I introduced that warning/behaviour.

**What does that mean for you?:** Basically nothing, the loader generated by glad will now contain some deprecated symbols which were removed in the core profile and are not used by GLES, but since compatibility is 100% compatible with core this is (hopefully) just a minor annoyance for you.

I like that IoT concept you mention.  The problem I'm aiming to solve is 'deprecation' and change in dbus specs.  So many 'little changes' happen that sooner or later Iot code in the field needs to be 'updated' to 'keep up with where it was'.   Now, the translation dictionary writer can ADD variables to dbus calls to include version numbers or defaults for programs that are 'old' that don't provide the necessary.  That way, code stays compatible without change and only the folks that want the value of the new thing need to cope with it.

Motions can be global rigid motions(can be handled with affine augmenters) or elastic deformation or bspline based local deformations. For the second one we don not have any augmentor. Typical example of nonrigid is deformation of muscle. Input will be an 3D image and output will be a newly generated data incorporated the desired nonrigid motion. There should be some mathematical formula or model, using which we could generate artificial motion data. But there is no standard formula, but I think it is possible to add some random bspline transform at different points on grid and generate local nonrigid motion OR we can use some force or energy gradient formula for simulating elastic deformations.

I was wondering if there is any package or software available to do this, but didn't find any for doing this nonrigid part.Actually if we can generate this, using this type of feature we can validate any registration methods or simulate different deformation models.

No likely related, but in [this file](https://github.com/0k/kids.test/blob/master/setup.py#L17) the
```python
try:
    from setuptools import setup
except ImportError:
    from distribute_setup import use_setuptools
    use_setuptools()
    from setuptools import setup
```
is not meaningful. `distribute` is deprecated and `distribute_setup` should not be used (the setuptools equivalent is ez_setup.py).  But this won't do anything anyways unless the bootstrap script in question (ez_setup.py) is included in your source distribution.

I understand the concern with using non-standard variable names, and fully agree that they should be avoided where possible. However, your arguments apply more to the current implementation than my proposal.

The change required is not an extension of the framework, but rather the removal of an unnecessary restriction. It does not change anything with respect to how PyEDA operates or how it would interact with external platforms. No additional translation would be required simply by reducing the restrictions. Any restrictions that apply to an external tool should be handled at that interface, not in PyEDA, as you also suggest. Interfaces always need error checking, and it's equally possible that an interfaced system might be more flexible and have more features rather than fewer.

I am merely proposing that the restrictions be adjusted to match the _actual requirements_ of PyEDA, so that I am not forced to add additional, nested, complex translation just to support something the system would be able to do with less than one line of modification already.

Hmm, let me check my understanding of what you're after. If we just want to fail the build if alerts of a given level are found, then you can use the available `--exit-code` param on the existing `alerts` command ([which defaults to true](https://github.com/Grunny/zap-cli/blob/master/zapcli/cli.py#L231-L232)) but it only allows failing based on ZAP's existing definition of the level (i.e. the default High for XSS, CSRF, etc.). If you're wanting to ignore a type of alert completely, you can also just disable that scanner ID (which you can do with either the `scanners` command or a param for the `quick-scan` command), which will stop the scan from including tests for that issue (which if you're going to ignore it anyway, can save some time on the scan). I think you're wanting this for passive scanning as well, so we can also easily add a command for turning passive scanners on and off (the `scanners` command only deals with the active scan issues).

What you can't do currently with `zap-cli` is change the alert level for a given issue ID, and you can't ignore an instance of an alert (i.e. you can't mark an issue on a specific URL/param/etc. to be ignored, only the given issue ID for all instances of that issue). So, is ignoring an instance of an issue on a specific URL/param/etc. what you want to do?

What I currently do in the CI infrastructure where I work is store the issues from previous active scans and have them marked as either false positives, or ticket filed/in progress/fixed/etc. so that subsequent scans will only alert on new issues or regressions so we don't get alert fatigue :). Something like that built into `zap-cli` could be useful, but we would need to think how it should work, perhaps being able to mark each issue as to be ignored and save that in a config file that can be used in future scans?

Failing all OIDs in presence of a single failed OID may represent some sort of compromise in embedded SNMP implementation. My theory is based on the design of SNMP v1 which has a single index (e.g. error-index) for pointing out failing OID. If more than one OID fail, SNMP v1 has no way to communicate that back to client. When implementing SNMP v2c, they may have decided to leave SNMP v1 logic in place just replacing error-index pointer with noSuchObject sentinel.

But that's just a theory, no hard evidence... ;-)

The hlapi thing was designed to remediate pysnmp usage complications. That gives me hope that it should be easer for you to accommodate. Here's a [quick link](http://pysnmp.sourceforge.net/docs/hlapi/asyncore/sync/manager/cmdgen/getcmd.html) to respective documentation page.

Let me revise my original answer them. Assuming you are calling `SNMPGet` from a thread and you are not doing SNMP I/O asynchronously, my suggestion is to use something like [this](http://pysnmp.sourceforge.net/examples/hlapi/asyncore/sync/manager/cmdgen/snmp-versions.html#snmpv2c):

    from pysnmp.hlapi import *
    from pysnmp.hlapi.lcd import CommandGeneratorLcdConfigurator

    class SNMPGet:
        """Instance of this class must be thread-local, not shared between threads."""
        def __init__(self, *):
            self._snmpEngine = SnmpEngine()

        def _internal_execute(self, tmp_object_identifiers):
            gen =  getCmd(self._snmpEngine,
                                   CommunityData('public'),
                                   UdpTransportTarget(('demo.snmplabs.com', 161)),
                                  ContextData(),
                                  *[ObjectType(ObjectIdentity(oid)) for oid in tmp_identifiers])

            errorIndication, errorStatus, errorIndex, varBinds = next(gen)

        def cleanup(self):
            """Running SnmpEngine instance against ever increasing set of targets may grow memory.
                To drop SnmpEngine's caches you can periodically call this method. The backside is
                slight drop in performance as hot cache will have to be repopulated.
            """
            CommandGeneratorLcdConfigurator.unconfigure(self._snmpEngine)

As a side note, if you are fetching many OIDs from many targetsl, [async I/O](http://pysnmp.sourceforge.net/examples/hlapi/asyncore/manager/cmdgen/snmp-versions.html#snmpv2c) may give you better performance compared to MT one.

When writing the original code I didn't expect that Python calls the cleanup on objects where `__init__` failed with an exception ([details on StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/a/6409718/997063)). Hence I assumed that `_handle` exists when cleaning up which results in the reported bug.

> open() in touch() fails due to non-existing directory, the irony is that touch() is probably supposed to be guarding against exactly this problem, but it turns out to be an easy but bad solution.

Actually `touch()` is used because the file is opened with `r+` mode which doesn't create a new file if it doesn't exist. It's fine that an exception is raised when the directory doesn't exist but it's not fine that cleanup fails in this case.

> I suggest opening an issue about it and questioning the existence of JSONStorage.__del__().

The more interesting issue here is how to do cleanup on storages. At the moment the concept isn't really clear. We have `db.close()` which calls `close()` on the storage. But users will probably forget to call it, so there is 1) a context manager which calls `close()` for you, and 2) JSONStorage implements `__del__` which calls `close()`. This of course may lead to bugs if the storage expects `close()` to be called just once. I'd be happy to revisit this topic for [v3.0](https://github.com/msiemens/tinydb/pull/69), but I'm not sure how to solve this in a elegant and solid way. What are your thoughts on this, @krisztianfekete?

Hmmm... interesting. I've never seen `None` as a result value.
For reference: the way the process name is determined right now works like this:

- `OpenHandle` -> `GetProcessImageFileNameW` (which returns the exe) -> `os.path.basename(exe)`: https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/39161251010503d6b087807c473f4fb648dfcbce/psutil/_psutil_windows.c#L616
- if that fails for permission errors we try `CreateToolhelp32Snapshot`: https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/39161251010503d6b087807c473f4fb648dfcbce/psutil/_psutil_windows.c#L647

The reason we have two implementations for the same thing is because the first method is faster (so we attempt it first) but it fails with `AccessDenied` for processes owned by another user in which case we fall back on using method #2.

The code you pasted shows we can potentially use a third method to determine the process name, that is `OpenHandle` -> `GetModuleFileNameEx` so I'll try to see how that works. My guess is it will be faster than using `CreateToolhelp32Snapshot`.

I'm not sure where `None` is coming from but I'm more keen to think it comes from `CreateToolhelp32Snapshot`. Can you verify that? What you should do is put some debugging prints in here https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/2c76acd569087d2f4a3dbb3ff438dcb1895f2027/psutil/_pswindows.py#L276, then reinstall. If you don't have Visual Studio (hence you cannot reinstall) you can figure out where `_pswindows.py` is located with:

```
>>> import psutil._pswindows
>>> psutil._pswindows  # this will give you the path
<module path ...>
```

...modify it in place, then re-run your code.

I will not add a `RedirectOutOfApiSubdomain` (or something like that) because, in my opinion, requesters to a web service should be blind to redirects and only interpret answers as they are sent back. Although it is reasonable to expect Bot API to not redirect, one can never say it *should not* redirect.

An easy way to differentiate between a bad token and server down is this:
- A bad token causes a redirect to https://core.telegram.org/bots and the resulting `BadHTTPResponse` has a status of 200.
- Server down would give you a `BadHTTPResponse` of status 502, as detailed in Issue #31

I could add a [redirect history](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/quickstart/#redirection-and-history) to `BadHTTPResponse` to help you differentiate. But, because situations giving rise to `BadHTTPResponse` are very limited and you can already distinguish them as described above, I would hold that off for now.

Your suggestion of adding subclasses to `TelegramError` is a good one. But I need you to give me **a list of possible errors and their error code**. I am sure you encounter those more than I ever do. Would you do that? I appreciate your help.

You shouldn't use `requests` for this (it's a synchronous library) and you don't need a temporary file for this (it's slow and can introduce race conditions).

Use aiohttp (included with discord.py) and BytesIO (part of the Python standard library) to achieve this:
```py
# near the top of your file with the rest of your imports
import aiohttp
from io import BytesIO
...
# in an async function, such as an on_message handler or command
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
    # note that it is often preferable to create a single session to use multiple times later - see below for this.
    async with session.get(url) as resp:
        buffer = BytesIO(await resp.read())

await client.send_file(message.channel, fp=buffer, filename="something.png")

# using a predefined ClientSession on the Client instance
client.session = aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=client.loop)  # then use client.session.get similar to above

# using a predefined ClientSession within a cog
def __init__(self, bot, ...):
    ...
    self.session = aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=bot.loop)
    ...
    # then use self.session.get similar to above

# 3.4 syntax
resp = yield from session.get(url)

try:
    content = yield from resp.read()
finally:
    resp.close()
buffer = BytesIO(content)

yield from client.send_file(message.channel, fp=buffer, filename="something.png")
```
Hope this helps.

Hi, please excuse my poor English, let me explain it again. my application is a server daemon, it create a producer when startup. first time, App got a request so it send out 100 messages for response. now there is no request incoming so the producer is in idle state; then, after about 30 minutes our App got a request, so it reuse the producer to send another 100 messages. note: this time, 1 failed and 99 succeeded I can use "retry feature" of producer to resend the 1 failed message, but the order of messages are changed. I think it's better if producer can take care of this situation: when socket is closed by remote server, kafka producer will send the failed message again. this will ensure send the 1 failed message first, because sometimes the order is important.

This is the group session timeout. If you dont call `consumer.close()` on exit, the group coordinator will not know whether the consumer has gracefully left or not. If not graceful exit, the group will wait for the duration of the session timeout before marking the consumer as "dead" and reallocating assigned partitions.

Is this still an issue? Another idea is that you may have a pending / blocking JoinGroupRequest ahead of your commit request. JoinGroupRequest can block on the server for up to the group's session_timeout. If that happens, you may end up with requests queued up behind it that timeout. Commit requests in particular would be impacted because both have to be sent to the same broker (the group coordinator).

After looking at this a little more, I think I can clarify the issue further. When using ajax with PHP and a database, the sort and filter parameters need to be part of the url query (not the hash) in order for PHP to use the parameters to sort and filter properly.

However, enabling `sort2hash` doesn't seem to change the url parameter in `pager_customAjaxUrl` that is passed from `pager_ajaxUrl`. For example, in the demo the url is `http://www.filltext.com/?callback=?` no matter what. This means the result will be unsorted and unfiltered when using PHP since there are no url query parameters to execute on. 

So it looks like when manipulating the url in `pager_customAjaxUrl` when `sort2hash` is enabled, the way the code currently works it would be better to start with `window.location.hash` instead of the url parameter. Then the hash part can be moved to the query part and manipulated further as needed.

If that is the best solution, then I can just implement that change and no fix is needed to your code. By the way, everything works fine when not fetching rows through ajax and PHP. I'll let you be the judge of the best way to proceed!

One of the biggest differences between iODBC and unixODBC is that the former is by default UTF-32 while the latter is UTF-16 for its Unicode APIs, and likewise any ODBC drivers used with them also need to have a compatible ABI. For example, ODBC Driver for SQL Server is UTF-16 only.

Looking at the trace it seems that the error returned from the server is:

DIAG [42000] [Microsoft][ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server][SQL Server]The server failed to resume the transaction. Desc:7700000001. (3971) 

DIAG [01000] [Microsoft][ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server][SQL Server]The transaction active in this session has been committed or aborted by another session. (3926) 

Unfortunately there are many things that can cause that, is it possible that something else is calling that stored procedure by the name? That would explain why renaming it might help.

You could make the use of SQLDescribeParam optional; i.e. the default is to bind with the string length (in UTF-16 code units, so this still needs to be fixed) but setting cursor.detect_metadata (or similar) option will cause it to use SQLDescribeParam. Of course, if setinputsizes() is set, that will override/bypass this.

Using SQLDescribeParam carries overhead (even for SQL Server) so makes sense if doing multi-row inserts; but for single-row, I think one of the other options will be better.

I don't remember exactly but I recall there is certainly a way to convert a string into UTF-16 and get its code-unit-length to pass into SQLBindParameter, so even if it uses exact string lengths, it will be the _correct_ exact string length... I will look into trying to fix this.

As you may notice, there is another Google Calendar module now... I'm working new changes and/or features into this module to make things more useful / exciting for the users.

It's all in good competition... and all in good fun too. 🥇 🥈 🥉 

Changes made recently...

* Add buttons to focus next/previous event, open event links, reset (eg clears out the scrolling), and refresh. We don't call Google API during any of those... except for refresh. Very nice.

* Add arguments to dictate the events... No more exposing the command.
    1) Don't show events that have started
    2) Don't show events that have been declined
    `arguments = ['nostarted', 'nodeclined']`

* By using `--details=all` too, we have more new placeholders.
* I `**e` everything in the dict too. Slightly more (useless) placeholders. Less lines too.

* As mentioned in earlier comment, we are already using `Tab Separated Values`.

* This module was initially simple as it does not expose end dates/times at all.

   1) Showing start date/time is more than enough for the users.
    2) I now added them. They need to be in their own formats for strftime to work ... The alternative is to use separate fancy separators to parse between them in single `format_event`.
   3) Users can show `1:00 AM - 3:00 AM`. They can modify things too... Showing `1 - 3` (via `remove = [':00 AM', ':00 PM']` option). Google have this option to trim an hour event.. so you can have `1 - 1:50 PM` on the bar still... Nice.

* When you scroll, you can use `is_focused` to expose additional information such as `{description}` (useful), `{location}` (possibly useful in some cases), creator `{email}` (meh), and `{calendar}` owner (meh). 

* No issues here right now in this commit. This is ready for review and/or merge.

Things I'd like to work on... eventually
* An `reminder` option (default 15min) + `urgent`.
* Cache offset and/or loop issues... mainly for reminding and urgent.... all without touching external `get_gcal_data` calls... If a first event is found to be less than `cache_timeout`, then we should use that one (minus reminder secs). Otherwise, back to `cache_timeout`.

Feedback welcome. Ty.

Oh.... That's a sneaky problem. `frozendict` never became a built-in, and we don't have access to `MappingProxyType` in Python 2. The solutions I can think of right now are:
- Add `frozendict` or something similar as a dependency
- Use a `namedtuple`
- Hand roll an immutable dictionary (this is what Flask/werkzeug opted to do, [implementation](https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/73683a2ee741288c18751a4f739b9758a9e18a55/werkzeug/datastructures.py#L1542))
- Use a `staticmethod` or `classmethod` getter (i.e. `self.get_default_config()`)

Thanks for the pull request! I agree with your reasoning about escaping curly brackets and ampersands. Those are definitely oversights on my part, and I'll work on patching it for the next release. However, I have a couple of thoughts about implementing this patch:

  1. It seems to me that the problem concerns rendering exclusively: we only think about whether we should escape characters when we are in the rendering phase, not when we are constructing the AST. For this reason, I think the escape attribute should not be introduced at token level, but instead at renderer level.

  2. I also think this is a problem that only affects LaTeX. The mistake I made was that I was trying to escape characters in the lstlisting environment, where I didn't need to. So we can pass in the information only when we need it, for example:

    ```python class LaTexRenderer(BaseRenderer): def render_block_code(self, token): inner = self.render_raw_text(token.children[0], escape=False) ... def render_raw_text(self, token, escape=True): return self.escape(token.content) if escape else token.content @staticmethod def escape(text): pass ```

    For HTML, we should escape characters regardless of whether they are in code blocks. Here's an improper example:

    ```html 
    
    PrimeCalc calc = new PrimeCalc();
    Func del = calc.GetNextPrime;
    ```

    Even though I think most browsers render <> symbols correctly, they should still be escaped so as not to be interpreted as HTML tags:

    ```html 
    
    PrimeCalc calc = new PrimeCalc();
    Func<int, int> del = calc.GetNextPrime;
    ```

    This is why I think the issue you raised is a LaTeX-specific problem.

I suspect limiting the changes to only `LaTeXRenderer` would also be easier to implement. Do you think you'd be interested in implementing it? If not, I can do it, and we can close this pull request and move the discussion to an issue instead. Let me know what you think!

理论上来说现在是不需要手动更新微信群聊的，但为了保持API稳定我还是保留了这个方法。
目前的群聊的调用基本来说是这样的：
* 获取方法为`get_chatrooms`，将会返回完整的群聊列表。
* 其中每个群聊为一个字典
* 传入update键为True将可以更新群聊列表并返回通讯录中保存的群聊列表，手动更新不会获取Uin
* 群聊列表为后台自动更新，如果中途意外退出存在极小的概率产生本地群聊消息与后台不同步
* 为了保证群聊信息在热启动中可以被正确的加载，即使不需要持续在线的程序也需要运行`itchat.run()`
* 如果不想要运行上述命令，请在退出程序前调用`itchat.dump_login_status()`，更新热拔插需要的信息

I tried adding a ``sphinx_cpp_tagname`` attribute to the signature nodes in jakobandersen/sphinx@0508d3d5b63f5576d06789ad8b6be1b8c37caccf.
The main line should have 'declarator' as tag, so the code from #239 could become something like the following (I haven't set up to test Breathe changes yet).
```Python
        for signode in node.children:
            try:
                tag = signode.sphinx_cpp_tagname
            except AttributeError:
                continue
            if tag = 'templateParams':
                pass
            elif tag = 'declarator':
                # Insert Doxygen target
                signode.insert(0, self.create_doxygen_target())
                # Update the actual signature
                self.update_signature(signode)
            else:
                # Unknown signature node
                pass
        # Add description to the last node, the content node.
        node.children[-1].extend(self.description())

This issue is misconceived. The current functionality supports the desired behavior e.g. with:
```
[bumpversion]
current_version = 0.3.0
commit = True
tag = True
parse = (?P<major>\d+)\.(?P<minor>\d+)\.(?P<patch>\d+)([.](?P<release>[gr][ac])[.](?P<rc>\d+))?
serialize = 
	{major}.{minor}.{patch}.{release}.{rc}
	{major}.{minor}.{patch}

[bumpversion:file:setup.cfg]
search = version = {current_version}
replace = version = {new_version}

[bumpversion:file:syapse_ontology/__init__.py]
search = __version__ = '{current_version}'
replace = __version__ = '{new_version}'

[bumpversion:part:release]
optional_value = ga
values = 
	rc
	ga

[bumpversion:part:rc]
first_value = 1
```

Then `bumpversion major|minor|patch` increases the part and moves to rc.1
followed by a `bumpversion rc` to move to rc.2 (and then rc.3 etc), or a `bump version release` to drop the `rc` part entirely.

@Hemanth2396 and @anilneeluri 

For large amount of data, it is recommended to write your corpus file. I think the below Q&A will answer your questions.

1. How to write a corpus file?
2. What is training and what are different training class are available?

### 1. How to write a corpus file?
Chatterbot does support both [YAML ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML) and JSON format in question and answers. You could find a large number of corpus files here https://github.com/gunthercox/chatterbot-corpus/tree/master/chatterbot_corpus/data.

Example

```YAML
- - text: Hello, how are you doing today?
  - text: I am doing well, thank you.
- - text: I cannot find my keys.
  - text: Where was the last place you remember having them?
```

### 2. What is training and what are different training class are available?
Training is the process involves loading example dialog into the chat bot’s database. Chatterbot does support different [training classes](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/training.html#training-classes) to train your bot.

From my experience, having very short utterances where it's mostly a slot value can easily lead to this situation. If you're going to do short variable (slot-based) utterances as responses to questions, you'll need an intent designed specifically for receiving the value and then using a session attribute to decide what logic to trigger.

In your case you might need to make [`AddChildIntent  add {Name}`](https://github.com/ThaWeatherman/alexa_skills/blob/master/infant/speech_assets/SampleUtterances.txt#L14) a separate intent related to a prompt for a name that might be used across other states of the app. (The other `AddChildIntent` utterances are probably ok since they include more words than just a slot.)

I ran into this design issue previously when prompting a user for selecting a particular podcast program to play. My case was simpler, so the "select" intent just called the function dealing with handling the "play" intent. The "select" intent was an utterance defined to be match when a user spoke just the program name. (Technically, at any time for any reason, they could just say the name of a correct program and it would start playing it as a result, but this would allow us the spot to put the check for session attributes to see the state of the dialog if needed.)

Just an aside: Flask-Ask has really nothing to do with the intent chosen by Amazon, if people are wondering.

The conversion works but the information lost is the duration. For example, if I ask Alexa "What is happening next weekend?" I get back a raw AMAZON.Date of "2017-W10-WE". And if I ask it "What is happening this Sunday?" I get back a raw AMAZON.Date of "2017-03-05".

But the first construct, implies multiple days (at least Saturday and Sunday and possibly part of Friday). So a corresponding date construct should capture this. It is either a start date and an end date or a start date and a duration. Technically you could argue that a day is also a start date and a duration but that is already captured in the data type.

I pulled out the relevant part of the convert code into a little script that illustrates the problem. You can see that "this weekend" and "Sunday" are equivalent after conversion and data about the original intent is lost.

`from __future__ import division, absolute_import, print_function, unicode_literals
import re
from datetime import datetime


_DATE_PATTERNS = {
    # "today", "tomorrow", "november twenty-fifth": 2015-11-25
    '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$': '%Y-%m-%d',
    # "this week", "next week": 2015-W48
    '^\d{4}-W\d{2}$': '%Y-W%U-%w',
    # "this weekend": 2015-W48-WE
    '^\d{4}-W\d{2}-WE$': '%Y-W%U-WE-%w',
    # "this month": 2015-11
    '^\d{4}-\d{2}$': '%Y-%m',
    # "next year": 2016
    '^\d{4}$': '%Y',
}

SINGLE_DAY_PATTERN = '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$'
YEAR_MONTH_PATTERN = '^\d{4}-W\d{2}$'
YEAR_PATTERN = '^\d{4}$'
WEEK_PATTERN = '^\d{4}-W\d{2}$'
WEEKEND_PATTERN = '^\d{4}-W\d{2}-WE$'
MONTH_PATTERN = '^\d{4}-\d{2}$'
YEAR_PATTERN = '^\d{4}$'
NOW_PATTERN = 'PRESENT_REF'


sample1 = "2017-W10-WE"
sample2 = "2017-03-05"
sample3 = "2017-03-04"

def to_date(amazon_date):
    # make so 'next decade' matches work against 'next year' regex
    amazon_date = re.sub('X$', '0', amazon_date)
    for re_pattern, format_pattern in list(_DATE_PATTERNS.items()):
        if re.match(re_pattern, amazon_date):
            if '%U' in format_pattern:
                # http://stackoverflow.com/a/17087427/1163855
                amazon_date += '-0'
            return datetime.strptime(amazon_date, format_pattern).date()
    return None


print(to_date(sample1))
print(to_date(sample2))
print(to_date(sample3))
print(to_date(sample1) == to_date(sample2)) # True

n = len(vertex)

All requests are currently HTTP by default, but can be switched to HTTPS by adding `secure=True` to your config. For example:

```
>>> from internetarchive import get_item
>>> item = get_item('my_identifier', config=dict(secure=True))
>>> item.protocol
'https:'
```

You can also add `secure: True` to your ia config file (i.e. `~/.config/internetarchive.yml`) to make all requests HTTPS by default.

In general, modularity optimization with Louvain method on Karate Club Graph gives 4 communities. Having 2 communities give a lower modularity than 4, and thus maximizing modularity here will not work. 

With Louvain Method, you cannot really specify the number of communities you want. You may try to play with the `resolution` parameters but it is more suited to get more communities than less.

You have 3 options:

  * Let the CMS main view do the URL handling. The CMS use the slug of the page to resolve automatically every page URLs
  * Let the CMS handle the URLs but delegate the rendering using your own view. Here is the documentation explaining how to do that: http://django-page-cms.readthedocs.org/en/latest/3rd-party-apps.html
  * You handle the URL yourself and use your own view. You should be able to do that without problems. All you need to do for all the templatetags to work properly is to make sure the context variables are present. Be sure to set those variables properly in your views:

  * current_page the page object,
  * path the path used to reach the page,
  * lang the current language,
  * pages_navigation the list of pages used to render navigation.

You made very good points, and in fact you made a good case that it might be better after all to simply maintain a good `docker-compose` file for managing the docker containers that must live after the test session.

On the other hand, I like the idea of being able to run the tests simply by calling `pytest` and let it handle fixture set-up and clean-up. In fact, there is already a [plugin](https://github.com/centralityai/pytest-docker-compose) for managing `docker-compose` files.

In both plugins, the containers are destroyed after the test. I think there is no way for `pytest` to know that you are done with your iterative testing session. Therefore, one might ask why would `pytest` create persistent Docker containers but not destroy it? Ideally the environment before and after the test should be kept the same.

Overall, I think my initial problem might be solved after the discussions we had here.

It is possible not to use `rpm-devel` for the install process.

Several ways to achieve it.
First of all, it is possible to install without `rpm-devel` after researching.
`rpm-libs` and `rpm-build-libs` are still needed.
But those are installed as a default, when I checked CentOS and Fedora container.

However using `rpm.pc` is more reliable than creating `rpm.pc` manually to predicting target condition.

So, I would try below logic to install.

1. Check if `rpm-devel` is installed. `rpm -q rpm-devel`.
1-1. If `rpm-devel` is installed, use it.
1-2. If `rpm-devel` is not installed, move on step 2.

2. Check current system is working by `dnf` or `yum`.

3. Check if the download plugin (feature) is available.

```
$ dnf download rpm-devel
```

or 

```
$ yum install --downloadonly --downloaddir=. rpm-devel
```

3-1. If the download plugin is installed, download `rpm-devel`, and extract and use `rpm-devel`.
3-2. If the download plugin is not installed, move on step 4.

4. manually create `rpm.pc`, and build and install it.
4.1. If the install is succeeded, the install process is done.
4.2. If the install is failed, the `rpm-py-installer` requires `rpm-devel`. And try to install with the `rpm-devel`.

In the mean time, you can write your own middleware that does the same thing:

```python
from django.utils.functional import SimpleLazyObject
from django_user_agents.utils import get_user_agent

def user_agent_middleware(get_response):
    def middleware(request):
        request.user_agent = SimpleLazyObject(lambda: get_user_agent(request))

        return get_response(request)

    return middleware
```

OK let's close or action this issue!

First it seems that the encoding error doesn't come from python, but from the encoding that the console is using. So the way to fix it is to run the command (in windows):

    chcp 65001

that sets the encoding to UTF-8 and then run Mammoth again. Or if working on pycharm, go to Settings>Editor>File Encodings and set the IDE and Project encodings accordingly.

[Source](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/31768128/pip-installation-usr-local-opt-python-bin-python2-7-bad-interpreter-no-such-f)

Now the issue of the symbols. (Which are not recognised by Mammoth)

Symbols are specified with the w:sym element within the w:r element. A symbol is a special character that does not use any of the run fonts specified in rFonts or in the style hierarchy. The character is determined by pulling the hexadecimal value specified in the char attribute from the font specified in the font attribute. The char attribute specifies the hexadecimal code for the Unicode character value of the symbol. The value can be stored in either of the following formats:

- directly in its Unicode character value from the font glyph or
- In a Unicode character value created by adding `F000` to the character value, thereby shifting the value into the Unicode private use area. This is done to allow interoperability with legacy word processing formats. So, if the value of the char attribute is F034, we would obtain the character value by removing F000 from F034 to obtain the character at the hexadecimal value 0x34 in the Wingdings font (or 52 as a decimal value).

Only Unicode characters are officially supported in HTML and only those should be used, as not all browsers will have fonts such as Wingdings and is outside the scope of Mammoth

It sounds we can do two things.
1. Keep Mammoth as is: ignore w:sym (Close issue)
2. Create a dictionary to convert the windings characters to unicode equivalents as good as we can. (Hairy! Not recommended.) [see list here](http://www.alanwood.net/demos/wingdings.html)
This issue was fixed in release 0.0.15

It would be a nice feature. I am trying to think of the ways how we can achieve this.

There are multiple ways to achieve this. But I would prefer to be able to do it with a single query.

We can always search keys starting with language code but other country code but this has some disadvantages.

* It would be painfully slow for large queries
* For instance `zh_cn` and `zh_tw` are basically two different writing styles for Chinese. They are not really interchangeable.

We can make another settings like  `settings.TRANSLATIONS_MAP` which contains values a list of strings containing interchangeable language keys:

```
TRANSLATIONS_INTERCHANGEABLE_KEYS = {
    'en_us': ['en_gb', 'en_au'],
    'en_gb': ['en_us', 'en_au'],
    ...
}
```

This way we can always do a `has_any` query and retrieve one of the interchangeable translations. That's the most efficient solution I can think of.

In response to your question about a passthrough; Yes!, that is possible.
Just register your application using the route_prefix `'/'` like so:

```
from flask import Flask, make_response

app = Sanic(__name__)

dispatcher = SanicDispatcherMiddlewareController(app)
flaskapp = Flask("MyFlaskApp")

# register the wsgi_app method from the flask app into the dispatcher
dispatcher.register_wsgi_application(flaskapp.wsgi_app, "/")

@flaskapp.route('/')
def index():
    return make_response("Hello World from Flask App")
```
In this example specifying the prefix `'/'` will cause all routes to be passed into the Flask application.

I can chime in for the rationale:

I ran into this with a ModelForm that had a ModelChoiceField with a ton of options, leading to a non-performant form when rendering those options with the default widget. My intended solution was to limit the fields (MyForm.Meta.fields) to those that I need, excluding the non-performant field in that case.

Then it turns out that even with the specified fields on the form, the mixin was still applying ``__all__``, causing that field I wanted to exclude to be included anyway.

I suppose you could specify the fields on the formset mixin, but that violates the DRY principles, since you've already defined them on the form itself.

Not expressing an opinion on solutions, however it seemed important to note the following:

1. The pickling/unpickle of storage within redis itself is probably not an issue, the ability for an attacker to modify the blob inside redis is predicated on their ability to access redis as well as know the secret key. At this point I think it's reasonable to say the application is well and truly compromised, typically applications are not built to defend themselves from a compromise of their backend data storage.

2. However, the key for the particular redis blob that is to be decoded is stored in the public facing cookie. This is done using pickle as well, via pyramid.session/signed_*serialize which makes use of a sha1 hmac for signing.

3. There is no way for an application to modify the behaviour of signed_*serialize other than monkeypatching, nor is it possible to instruct redis_sessions to use a different serialization option, so typical app developers cannot mitigate this by switching serialization methods.

A proposal to replace the hardcoded serialization exists here: https://github.com/ericrasmussen/pyramid_redis_sessions/issues/38 - webob.SignedCookieProfile uses sha512 and json serialization by default.

Very good question, Rama.

To answer your question, we need to look further on how we are gonna use the WebDriverWait.until/until_not method. The first argument of WebDriverWait will be passed in to the predicate method/function of until/until_not. The value passed in as the first argument of the WebDriverWait will depend on what the predicate method/function would need to process. Hence you saw there are variation of values being used.

For example: wait_for_async_condition method would need to execute async Javascript. To execute async Javascript function, we would need the browser instance. Thus we need to passed in the browser instance as the WebDriverWait first argument. WebDriverWait will then passed in that browser instance to the lambda function and we would be able to execute the async Javascript from inside the lambda function.

Hope that makes sense.

Hi @ghcs27, great to see all your recent contributions.

I like the idea of this PR however I am trying to think how people would use it. 

There are a few more prefixes (full list I know of shown below) which is not always just the prefix - more the start of the uri.

URI prefix | Source
------------ | -------------
x-rincon-stream:  | Line In
x-sonos-htastream: | TV
x-file-cifs: | Local Library
x-rincon-mp3radio: | Radio
x-sonosapi-stream:| Radio
aac: | Radio
x-sonosapi-radio: | Radio
hls-radio: | Radio
http: | Track or Radio 
https: | Track or Radio
x-sonos-spotify: | Spotify
x-sonos-http: | Google (music library or mix)
x-sonos-http:library | Amazon (my music)
x-sonosapi-hls-static: | Amazon (tracks and radio stations)
x-sonos-http:ondemand_track | Napster tracks
x-sonosprog-http:station_track | Napster stations

On a Sonos controller music is shown with one of the following display formats and controls - I believe determined by the URI:

Controller format | Display
------------ | -------------
Radio format| Shows the name of the radio station and other available data. No seek, next, previous, or voting capability. Examples: TuneIn, radioPup
Smart Radio|  Shows track name, artist, and album. Limited seek, next  and sometimes voting capability depending on the Music Service.  Examples: Amazon Prime Stations, Pandora Radio Stations.
Track format| Shows track name, artist, and album the same as when playing from a queue. Full seek, next and previous capabilities. Examples: Spotify, Napster, Rhapsody.
Line in| with no controls
TV| with TV controls

If its purpose is to show what's displayed then we could restrict it to the 5 display types.

The other use is for stopping and starting or re-starting play - as in snapshot. Here the key things to know are is it being played from TV, Line_in, a Queue, a Cloud Queue (Alexa started queue!), or a Stream, or in fact a Slave to another player. You can't always get this information from the uri alone and have to use .avTransport.GetMediaInfo to get the URI of the music source. 

Sorry for the long ramble, I welcome thoughts on what we should be presenting as values, what these then mean, and how people can then use it. 

Cheers David

Hi, you are correct that it's not officially documented and therefore not a part of our public API.  In practice these handlers don't change and the specific handler in question has been connected to that event name for 5+ years so it's probably safe.

However if you wanted a more reliable way to do this that goes through our public API, you could register your own [event handler](http://botocore.readthedocs.io/en/latest/topics/events.html#event-types) that pulls out the base64 contents.  It's more code, but it should always work:


```python

import base64

import botocore.session


class ConsoleOutputRetriever(object):
    def __init__(self, session):
        self._client = session.create_client('ec2')
        self._client.meta.events.register_first(
            'after-call.ec2.GetConsoleOutput',
            self._extract_raw_console_output
        )
        self._console_output = ''

    def get_console_output(self, instance_id):
        self._client.get_console_output(
            InstanceId=instance_id
        )
        return self._console_output

    def _extract_raw_console_output(self, parsed, **kwargs):
        base64_output = parsed['Output']
        self._console_output = base64.b64decode(base64_output)


s = botocore.session.get_session()
c = ConsoleOutputRetriever(s)
print(c.get_console_output('i-MYINSTANCEID'))


```

It breaks the contract of `is_compatible`. I am -1 on having code that is deliberately incorrect. It makes adding additional handlers brittle because the point we place them in the resolver chain impacts whether or not they will ever be called. It doesn't give helpful information because even in the best case it only tells you the error from the most recent handler, so you only get a subset of information. Relying solely on `hasattr('write')` is incorrect because calling `.write` on the following implementation of `io.RawIOBase` raises `NotImplementedError`:

```python
class NonWriteableFile(io.RawIOBase):
    def writeable(self):
        return False
```

Thus surfacing a python bug that we could easily avoid.

With a little effort to customize the `deis-controller`, you _might_ be able to successfully deploy applications to an external k8s cluster.  Among the things that would _not_ work, however, would be logging and routing.

For routing to work, `deis-router` instances need to share an etcd cluster with the k8s cluster so they can discover the service addresses for your pods.  Each `deis-router` instance also needs to be co-located with a `kube-proxy` instance that will route and load balance traffic _from_ that `deis-router` to an application pod.

Deis application logging relies on `deis-logspout` being co-located with your application containers so it can attach to their `STDOUT` and forward each line to the platform's internal log aggregator (i.e. `deis-logger`) or elsewhere (e.g. Papertrail).

All-in-all, you _could_ do it, but in the end, you wouldn't be getting the real Deis experience.  You'd be using it only for deployment, but relying on the services of the k8s cluster you already have in place for things like routing and logging.  While it may technically be doable, it's likely more trouble than it's worth in terms of the complexity that fragmented infrastructure would promote.

> Error: 100: Key not found (/deis/registry/masterLock) 

That is actually from registry's boot script, which updates that etcd key in loop while the registry has an open listen port. It looks likely that the boot script failed to communicate with etcd temporarily, which can cause `exit 1` and a container restart. 

But it looks like registry instead received a SIGHUP, which means an etcd key under /deis/registry or /deis/store changed, or perhaps that the key became available again after a communication failure. This shouldn't generally be the case while provisioning, but etcd can be very sensitive to network conditions.

Cleaning u old issues, reopen if this is still active.

- [ ] Help text ('-h') shows 'Create or update a virtual machine'. That is wrong, nothing to do with VMs.
- [ ] Allows me to create or update (operations on existing resources are successful). Given that we have a separate command for updates, should this command be create only, i.e. use 'If-none-match *' request header? At the least we need to give the option of create-only vs create-or-update
- [ ] Supports '--location -l' with default 'global'. Given that the only supported value for DNS zones is 'global', this parameter is useless and should be removed.
- [ ] In the help text, --name -n is described as "The name of the zone without a terminating dot." Whilst the service cares about terminating dots, the CLI should not. I.e. the text should be "The name of the zone, for example 'contoso.com'." And if a terminating dot is provided, the CLI should simply silently remove it before calling the service. In this way we optimize for usability by humans.

Repro steps given here for convenience:

1. Login to the Azure Portal

```bash
az group create --name "bssgroupcli3" --location "westus"

az iot device create --resource-group "bssgroupcli3" --hub-name "bssiotcli3" --device-id "coffeepot"
az iot hub policy list --resource-group "bssgroupcli3" --hub-name "bssiotcli3"
az iot hub policy create --resource-group "bssgroupcli3" --hub-name "bssiotcli3" --name "coffeeclient" --permissions ServiceConnect
az iot hub policy list --resource-group "bssgroupcli3" --hub-name "bssiotcli3"
```

4. Note that the output of the first call to "az iot hub policy list" displays all of the default SAS Policies for a hub (iothubowner, service, device, etc.).

When you read bcolz object from disk, your data is actually not loaded to memory except the leftovers which are actually loaded into memory as a numpy array, that's why it is so fast to open an on-disk carray.

If you have a look at the memory consumed by `arr7 = load_bcolz("'data/bcolz'")` you will see is way much less than your compressed data, this is because your data is still on disk, only loaded when needed, let's say for example when you [:] or [1] or maybe use an iterator.

I believe `clevel = 1 ` should give you nice speeds but compress ratio will be worse

http -j --auth 'user:pass2' POST localhost:8888/v1/batch requests:='[{"method":"PUT","path":"/buckets/default/collections/my-collection/records/my-record","body":{"data":{"my-field": "my-value2"}}}]'
```

My argument is that whoever authored the plugin doesn't care whether the events came from someone making a batch request with two PUTs, or someone making two separate PUT requests. The events should be the same, and have the same semantics, in both cases. So if you want to remove the `record_id` from the second case, you should remove it from the first case too. But maybe I'm missing a point somewhere.

For those who happen upon this issue: it turns out that the problem was caused by the presence of `pycosat 0.6.0` in your environment, installed by `pip`. In fact, `conda` requires 0.6.1. Normally, that is the version that would be installed when updating to `conda 4.x`, but for some reason that was not happening, possibly because `pycosat` was pip installed. We're going to add a fix to `conda` so that it still operates even with `pycosat 0.6.0`.

Does `conda list`, for `<pip>` entries, display the Python distribution name or the python package name(s)?

I guess this begs the question, if conda is python agnostic, why are pip installed packages displayed at all in conda list? For example, why doesn't it also list linux distribution package manager installed python packages (or any other software, for that matter).

@irobiul Your issue is not related to the current issue, it is about failing to find GTK - Image viewer or whatever equivalent lib works for your OS.
```cv2.imshow('Amanda',img)``` is causing the issue.

.. also, forcing the user to add this to their PATH results in that PATH always being present:

Here I created a new env, `test_env` and ran `source activate test_env`, but I still also have the root env on my PATH (`/Users/ray/mc-x64-2.7/bin`). My final PATH is:

`/Users/ray/mc-x64-2.7/envs/test_env/bin:/Users/ray/mc-x64-2.7/bin:/usr/local/texlive/2016/bin/x86_64-darwin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/opt/X11/bin:/usr/X11/bin:/Library/TeX/texbin`

This will cause all sorts of behavior changes (most worrying for me, when `conda-build` runs its tests it will find dependencies from the root env and return a false positive).

I'm not *necessarily* -1 on having root always on PATH in general (I am undecided) but I don't think it should be done as a side-effect of supporting less good shells and I definitely think it must be optional (for example, during testing).

@kalefranz, I've rebased this against the latest master. I'm exercising it by performing a variety of clone operations, including:

- Cloning environments with identically named packages from different channels. As I alternate between source environments, conda properly re-downloads the packages with the name collision.
- Cloning environments with packages from channels that are not on the channel list. Conda properly includes those channels in any index downloads, instead of relying on the `.condarc` channel list.
- Cloning environments where all packages are present in the cache. No internet downloads of any kind (index or package) are scheduled, which is a Nice Thing.

So I say this is good to go, but @ilanschnell might want to try some of his wacky environments.

As for ```ChunkedOutputBuffer#write``` because of ```while to_write > 0:``` and ```wrote = min(to_write, remaining)``` I think the condition ```remaining < to_write <= max_chunk_size``` may be true only on the first iteration and will cut short the existing chunk (I did not include this because I could not see any case this would be useful -- the old code cuts short the existing/last new chunk because it does ```if new_data_size > chunk_remaining: self.chunk()``` without filling ```chunk_remaining```). Also, ```self.chunk()``` without ```remaining = max_chunk_size``` will waste one iteration (3 comparisons and 2 ops)

I just noticed the junk code I've committed:
```python
data_size = self._end - self._start
if data_size > new_data_size:
    new_end = self._end + new_data_size
    self._data[self._end:new_end] = bytearray(data_size)
```
I was trying to pre-calculate the size for `_data` before the actual writes to avoid multiple extends (did not finish, hope to do so as time permits). 

From what I saw [here](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/e2f92de6a90ae11a6d8e01bd17fd88b005516835/Objects/bytearrayobject.c#L1680), bytearrays are stored in contiguous blocks of memory and even though Python tries to [overallocate](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/e2f92de6a90ae11a6d8e01bd17fd88b005516835/Objects/bytearrayobject.c#L227) for small changes this still implies a _huge_ overhead for the allocs & [copy](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/e2f92de6a90ae11a6d8e01bd17fd88b005516835/Objects/bytearrayobject.c#L248) required for large data. 

Resizing ```_data``` in one shot before the chunked write starts (recursive or not) should fix the memory issue by using one extend per ```write``` call instead of one per chunk required for the data --- in the worst case. The performance gain should also be significant because the old data will remain in place.

I don't fully understand this issue.

``singlediode`` returns multiple kinds of data. The pvlib style is to return labeled data in these situations. That means a DataFrame when the returned data is 2D and the input is a Series or DataFrame; OrderedDict if not. ``singlediode`` handles at least some kinds of numpy 2D+ inputs that I've used just fine. So, my experience with ``singlediode`` is that it already handles broadcasting for reasonable inputs. Maybe someone posted specific example of where it didn't do what you expected and I missed it in the flurry of discussion. Either way, could you (re)post it here?

``i_from_v`` and ``v_from_i`` return one kind of data each, so they don't need the labeling machinery. I am surprised to read that they broadcast to the smallest of ``voltage``, ``photocurrent``, or ``resistance_shunt``. That sounds like a bug. Perhaps that's due to something about ``np.broadcast_arrays`` that I don't understand (I don't have any experience with this function). Again, reproducible examples in this issue would help me to keep track of the status.

The problem here is most likely caused by using `update`: It will replace the value of the 'datasource' key and *not* recursively apply "update" if the value is a dictionary.

A working example can be created by adding
```python
SETTINGS['DOMAIN']['people']['datasource']['projection']['lastname'] = 0
```
to [trivial.py](https://github.com/pyeve/eve-sqlalchemy/blob/5e6177500623be2312b8e0670a450f98c19d6608/examples/trivial.py).

But be aware that fields hidden this way can still be read by applying the projection as query parameter:
```sh
curl -g 'http://localhost:5000/people?projection={"lastname":1}'
```

Quick update:

We chatted about this and realized that, although it's possible to use a transport mechanism directly (such as Paramiko's API or command-line SSH via the subprocess module), we really want to use something a little higher level. Ansible seems like a reasonable solution, given that it's a Python package with a proper API and it's purpose for existence is dead-simple system management.

I sat down today to play around with Ansible and see if I could make it work. Ansible needs to know which hosts to contact, and by default, it uses `/etc/ansible/hosts`. I decided to make a file at `~/.config/pulp_smash/hosts`:

    localhost              ansible_python_interpreter=/usr/bin/python2  ansible_connection=local
    pine.ichimonji10.name  ansible_python_interpreter=/usr/bin/python2

I uninstalled ansible from my system as a whole, and created a suitable virtualenv like so:

```sh
virtualenv -p python2 env2
source env2/bin/activate
pip install ansible
```

With this, it's possible to contact remote hosts:

```
(env2)[ichimonji10@beech:tmp]$ ansible all --inventory-file ~/.config/pulp_smash/hosts --module-name shell --args 'echo foo'
localhost | success | rc=0 >>
foo

pine.ichimonji10.name | success | rc=0 >>
foo
```

The `--inventory-file` argument can also be specified as an environment variable, which is perfect for us. Cool! We have a clean, powerful and orthogonal separation of responsibilities.

You'll notice, however, that I explicitly have to create a Python 2 virtualenv on the local system and use a Python 2 interpreter on each target system. This is because Ansible is currently only compatible with Python 2, and it expects `/usr/bin/python` to be a Python 2 executable. There is significant work being done to put out Ansible v2. You can get a sense by seeing the number of issues under the [v2 milestone](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/milestones/v2). You can also read https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/10771 and https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/1409 for more information.

I've seen mention of the ability to use a v2 branch or otherwise get access to the v2 code. If so, that'd be awesome. But at this point, I'm not sure what's involved in using v2 code.

My thoughts on this, all of which should be fixed for the next release:

1. Headers:

Any file that existed before the dmlc->apache move should have two headers. The old one and the standard ASF one. Any file that was created after the code move should have only the standard ASF header. Note that this is high level, there may be a file that has other types of src headers, like maybe a piece of BSD code was copied in etc. 

2. License files. All these should be fixed. 

--

On your questions:

1: I don't think we should be changing those two files. The files are currently in dmlc and not in apache. They are a style-bug in dmlc, and we do have the question of whether we should be pulling this code into mxnet as source the way we do.

2: I believe this means putting back "* Copyright (c) 2015 by Contributors" (or sometimes a different year), wherever that was deleted in https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/7379

3: It does look confusing. It looks like it came in on this pull request pre-Apache days: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/891 . As it was a contribution to an Apache 2.0 licensed project, the contribution would be under Apache 2.0. It looks like it is a specific version of the DMLC "Copyright Contributors" that is listing the 3 contributors who worked on the pull request. I would treat it the same as you do other DMLC files that say Copyright, but in this case list the three people instead of 'Contributors'.

Hi @ashb , got your point.

But to my understanding, `Airflow` is using Python built-in package `imp` to import DAG files, which would work for uppercase extension python scripts.

Using the examples you provided above.
```
>>> import imp
>>> imp.load_source("test", "foo.PY")
hi upper case
<module 'test' from 'foo.PY'>
```

```
import imp
>>> imp.load_source("test", "foo.py")
hi lower case
<module 'test' from 'foo.py'>
```

or even

```
>>> imp.load_source("test", "foo.Py")
hi mixed case
<module 'test' from 'foo.Py'>
```

So `Airflow` itself can accept & import DAG file with extension containing UPPER cases. However due to the reason I described earlier, `Airflow` ignores and misses them. To users, this issue may be very misleading, because `python foo.PY` (NOT `python -m foo.PY`) would definitely work.

Using that I've added a specific x64 override as well do no matter your environment variable order it'll pick the right version for the platform if you specify the platform. ```<Project>
  <PropertyGroup 
      Condition="'$(OS)' == 'Windows_NT' and
                 '$(PlatformTarget)' == 'x86' and
                 '$(TargetFrameworkIdentifier)' == '.NETCoreApp' and
                 '$(SelfContained)' != 'true'"
                  >
    <RunCommand>$(MSBuildProgramFiles32)\dotnet\dotnet</RunCommand>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <PropertyGroup
    Condition="'$(OS)' == 'Windows_NT' and
                 '$(PlatformTarget)' == 'x64' and
                 '$(TargetFrameworkIdentifier)' == '.NETCoreApp' and
                 '$(SelfContained)' != 'true'"
                  >
    <RunCommand>$(ProgramW6432)\dotnet\dotnet</RunCommand>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

On Tue, Nov 10, 2015 at 2:28 PM, Thomas Pickert <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> @erikzenker <https://github.com/erikzenker> Not a maintainer either, but
> I'm fairly familiar with the Brotli specification, albeit from a *decoder*
> perspective rather than an encoder.
>
> I am surprised that it should be possible to parallelize metablock
> encoding. It is possible that my understanding is incorrect. I will try to
> explain why I think that parallel encoding can't work with Brotli.
>
> In Brotli, there are three mutable concepts that get initialized at the
> start of the stream, and then live across metablock boundaries. They have
> an impact on the decoding behavior of each block, and they also potantially
> get mutated within each block.
>
> The three concepts are:
> 1. The output window (size defined by WSIZE in the stream header),
> initialized as empty. Used for back references, can span multiple blocks.
> 2. The literals buffer of the last two literals, referred to as p1 and p2
> in the specification, initialized as p1 = 0, p2 = 0. Used to calculate
> Context ID for literals.
> 3. The distances (ring) buffer of the last four distances, initalized as
> [4, 11, 15, 16], where 4 is the most recent distance. Used in the decoding
> of distances, where distance codes can refer to these values.
>
> As far as I understand it, parallel decoding of blocks is not possible
> because of those concepts, since subsequent bytes may depend on the state
> of any of these three concepts, which might have been mutated by actions on
> earlier compressed data.
>
> Unfortunately, all of the test cases that actually contain multiple
> metablocks are structured in a way that doesn't test for this. I.e. in all
> of those cases, all of the output data is really encoded in just one of the
> metablocks. Because of that, the three stream-wide concepts above do not
> actually get mutated in those test cases, and thus your parallel
> implementation passes, when really, it should conceptually fail.
>
> @szabadka <https://github.com/szabadka> Am I overlooking something?
>
Each thread of the parallel implementation can have access to the whole
input data, therefore each of them has access to 1. the window (which is
just past input data) and 2. to the last two bytes of the previous input
block.

To address 3. the parallel encoder has to ensure that it will not use the
last distances to create a distance short code for the first two commands
of the meta-block (it is the choice of the encoder whether to make use of
the distance ringbuffer of to just output raw distances). After the first
two commands, the distance ringbuffer is fully initialized based on the
current meta-block and the encoder can start using it.

So yes, it is possible to do parallel metablock encoding.

With this PR, the headers are placed into `PYTHON_INCLUDE_DIRS/pybind11` while the CMake config files are going into `CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX`. This isn't going to work because CMake expects that the headers and config files are placed consistently relative to `CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX`. The `find_package` tests are failing because they're testing expected CMake behavior and I don't think this should be modified.

A better way to do this would be to just give CMake a different `CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX` so that everything is still consistent. But this isn't compatible with the current way pybind11's `setup.py` installs headers into `include/pythonx.x/pybind11`. An alternative would be to adopt a numpy-style header location: `site-packages/pybind11/include`. The nice thing about this is that the CMake config files could also be installed into `site-packages/pybind11/share/cmake`. On the CMake side, the change then becomes trivial: `CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=site-packages/pybind11` just needs to be passed as an argument. This would keep everything nice and consistent. I was thinking about proposing this, but the only downside is that it would complicate `setup.py` and potentially add `cmake` as an install dependency.

Note that switching the header install location from `include/pythonx.x/pybind11` to `site-packages/pybind11/include` would also resolve this PyPy issue: #596.

The version above will cause problems if called with, for example, an `array_t<T>`: it'll match the template and call that instead of calling the proper `array(object &o)` constructor [added by the PYBIND11_OBJECT_CVT macro].

If we want this (I'm fairly neutral on it), this constructor works better: it's simpler in that it just maps array(1,2,3) to array({1,2,3}), but more importantly it won't match constructors called with arguments that aren't implicitly convertible to `size_t`:

```C++
    template <typename... Ix, typename = detail::enable_if_t<detail::all_of<std::is_convertible<Ix, size_t>...>::value>>
    explicit A(Ix... dimensions) : A{{ (size_t) dimensions... }} {}
```

The issue appears to be that whenever a thread acquires the GIL, it checks whether the Python interpreter has started finalizing yet, and immediately drops the GIL and exits the thread if so:
```
void
PyEval_RestoreThread(PyThreadState *tstate)
{
    if (tstate == NULL)
        Py_FatalError("PyEval_RestoreThread: NULL tstate");
    assert(gil_created());

    int err = errno;
    take_gil(tstate);
    /* _Py_Finalizing is protected by the GIL */
    if (_Py_IsFinalizing() && !_Py_CURRENTLY_FINALIZING(tstate)) {
        drop_gil(tstate);
        PyThread_exit_thread();
        Py_UNREACHABLE();
    }
    errno = err;

    PyThreadState_Swap(tstate);
}
```
This is in many respects exactly what you want - if you're in the middle of finalization, it's not safe to keep running Python code. But there's a subtlety with respect to how thread exit is implemented, at least on Linux: it basically uses a magic exception which can't be caught (that's the ForcedUnwind business) but still runs all destructors and such. As thread exit unwinds past time_sleep (which originally dropped the GIL), nothing happens, because time_sleep is implemented in C and doesn't use RAII for its GIL management; then as thread exit unwinds past py::gil_scoped_acquire (which originally acquired the GIL), the gil_scoped_acquire destructor tries to release the GIL and finds it doesn't have the GIL to release (because PyEval_RestoreThreads already released it and some other thread, in this case the main thread, has since acquired it). So you get an exception thrown during stack unwinding, which automatically calls terminate(). Bang, you're dead.

If you're curious, Python 2.7 doesn't have the exit-when-reentering-Python-during-finalization logic because it created an issue with some third-party module and people were generally reluctant to touch Python 2.7 finalization too much: https://bugs.python.org/issue21963

One way to get around this would be to ensure that the GIL is not released during interpreter finalization, something like:
```
diff --git a/include/pybind11/pybind11.h b/include/pybind11/pybind11.h
index a04d536..67badd1 100644
--- a/include/pybind11/pybind11.h
+++ b/include/pybind11/pybind11.h
@@ -1831,6 +1831,20 @@ public:
     }

     PYBIND11_NOINLINE ~gil_scoped_acquire() {
+        // On Python 3, trying to acquire the GIL during interpreter finalization
+        // will immediately call pthread_exit(), which unwinds the stack. Suppose
+        // our stack looks like:
+        // - some C++ function with a gil_scoped_acquire in scope
+        // - some Python C API function that releases the GIL
+        // - some Python C API function (the same or different) that acquires the GIL
+        // If the last acquisition notices finalization and starts unwinding,
+        // we'll reach the destructor of gil_scoped_acquire without holding the
+        // GIL, because unwinding past a point where the GIL was released doesn't
+        // re-acquire the GIL if the release happened from C code. This is not
+        // an error and we shouldn't do anything in that case. If we *do* hold the
+        // GIL, of course we must still release it, finalization or not.
+        if (!Py_IsInitialized() && detail::get_thread_state_unchecked() != tstate)
+            return;
         dec_ref();
         if (release)
            PyEval_SaveThread();
```
I'd be curious if you still see the issue with the above patch applied to your pybind11.

This is an older version of the MongoDB driver.

In 2.12.2, \gluon\packages\dal\CHANGES says:

```
Version 15.03
-------------

Released on March 23rd 2015
```

Given this:

```

db.define_table('tt', Field('aa'), Field('bb', default='bb'))

db.tt.insert(aa='1111', bb='a')

db.tt.insert(aa='1112', bb='b')

db.tt.insert(aa='1112', bb='c')

db().select(db.tt.aa, db.tt.bb, distinct=db.tt.aa)

```

Which db.tt.bb will be coupled to the 'distinct' one of db.tt.aa ? It basically depends on the backend used.


```distinct=db.tt.aa``` works on postgres, which has some limit in selecting fields in a group by clause. For example on postgres the following works:

```

db().select(db.tt.aa, db.tt.bb, distinct=db.tt.aa)

```

while this is not allowed:

```

db().select(db.tt.aa, db.tt.bb, distinct=db.tt.aa)

```

On other backends (es: sqlite) both queries return something.


Given these premises, my advice is that if you want to run distinct on a specific field you should use groupby. For what concern mongodb, I would go with the basic approach ```distinct=db.tt.aa```

```
res = db(db.auth_user.id > 0).select(limitby=(0,1000), reusecursor=True)
…
res = db(db.auth_user.id > 0).select(limitby=(1000, 2000),
                                             reusecursor=db['_lastcursor'])
```
Seems to work as expected

```python
# patch zmq garbage-collection Thread to use green Context:
from zmq.utils.garbage import gc
gc.context = zmq.Context()
```

agreed. Well, no objection from me at any rate. I guess we can reuse `as_tuple`. It may get a little tricky in combination with the `c01b` ordering in `Conv2DCCLayer` when `dimshuffle=False`, but it should be doable nevertheless.

> This means `standardize` can't accept Theano expressions

Really? Why? `np.array(1) / some_expression` should be a valid Theano expression. Same goes for the bias. Is there anything I'm missing?

> are there any ways to output the intermediate variable of a layer?

Only the ways offered by Theano, but there's no need to change the source code of a layer for that. If you want to see an intermediate output in addition to the output at the end of a network, just compile a function for that:
```python
output, intermediate = lasagne.layers.get_output([network, network.input_layer.input_layer.input_layer], deterministic=True)
pred_fn = theano.function([input_var], [output, intermediate])
```
Alternatively, for debugging, the test value system may come handy:
http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/tutorial/debug_faq.html#using-test-values
When you activate it (`THEANO_FLAGS=compute_test_value=warn`), you can assign a test value to the input variable: `input_var.tag.test_value = np.random.randn(*input_shape)`, and then obtain the outputs of all layers at once: `outputs = lasagne.layers.get_output(lasagne.layers.get_all_layers(network))`. Every expression in that list will now have a `tag.test_value` you can inspect to see what's going on in the network.
When using test values, you can also start a `pdb` prompt right before calling `get_output` and then step through the code to investigate what's happening inside each `get_output_for()` method of a layer.

Hope this helps! I don't see how we could extend Lasagne for this, so I'm closing this issue. Feel free to continue the discussion here.

I believe we have resolved the "missing message" problem, but we continue to have an "extra message" problem. We send over 40k messages and somehow 40k + [1,2,3,4,5,6, or so] extra messages end up in our second topic.

Here is the theory:

Typically we start up 4 processes from our main consumer script. There are 16 partitions on the topic we are consuming. We are thinking that the process goes something like this:

Process A starts up and takes ownership of partitions 0-15 and begins consuming
Process B starts up and some rebalancing mechanism kicks off giving Process B partitions 0-7 and and Process A partitions 8-15 (just an example)
....so on and so on for Process C and D

We think that during this rebalancing process that a handful of messages are slipping through to other processes because their respective offsets havent updated yet.

If you reference the code in the link, it would be somewhere between "for message in consumer:" and "consumer.commit_offsets()" where this extra message problem happens. 

Does this sound like a plausible theory?

* Mailjet's v3 send API apparently can't handle commas in display-names in the To/Cc/Bcc params. Commas work fine in Recipients...Name params, so this is only a problem when the message is using Cc or Bcc. But the failure is problematic: the message appears to succeed, but isn't actually sent.

* I'm considering changing Anymail's `tags` from Mailjet's CustomID param to their CustomCampaign param. Mailjet [suggests CustomID](https://dev.mailjet.com/mandrill/#tags) for migrating from Mandrill tags, but I think they misunderstand how tags are typically used. From what I can tell, there's no way in MJ's dashboard to get reporting by CustomID, but you can pull up CustomCampaign-grouped statistics pretty easily.

I had this same question, whether `points in region` should / could work for array-valued points.

There's some information here what `in` really does:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/12244074/python-source-code-for-built-in-in-operator
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/37613288/in-statement-for-lists-tuples-of-floats

I used this example to play around:
```python
import numpy as np

class PixCoord:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y
    
    def isscalar(self):
        return np.isscalar(self.x)

class PixelRegion:
    def __init__(self, center, radius):
        self.center = center
        self.radius = radius
    
    def __contains__(self, coord):
        dx = self.center.x - np.array(coord.x)
        dy = self.center.y - np.array(coord.y)
        d = np.sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy)
        mask = d < self.radius
        print(mask)
        return mask
        
        
c = PixCoord(x=1, y=2)
c2 = PixCoord(x=[1, 2], y=[2, 2])
r = PixelRegion(center=PixCoord(0, 0), radius=3)
print(c in r)
print(c2 in r)
```

The `c2 in r` statement results in
```
ValueError: The truth value of an array with more than one element is ambiguous. Use a.any() or a.all()
```
without a further traceback what happened.
I think effectively Python is doing something like calling `bool(retval)` on the return value of `__contains__`, doing this manually `return bool(mask)` gives the same error.

So my conclusion is the same one as @joleroi's -- making `in` work for arrays of points is not possible (or if it is, I presume it's not a great idea).

I'm not sure it's a good idea if having `__contains__` in addition to `contains` with a subset of the functionality is useful. My suggestion would be to remove it and have one and only one way to do it: `region.contains(coords)`. But I don't care strongly, so I'm not re-opening this issue for discussion.
