This is largely on the right track with a couple of caveats:

1. Rather than using multiple inheritance in the test cases, you should just have the tests inherit from `ConfigTestCase` unless absolutely necessary. 
2. You probably want to use subdirectories of the temporary directory for the config, log, and work directories. I can see the directories interacting in ugly ways otherwise.
3. You may want to consider solving #4435 first. To build on my description in #4434, the config object is [built at runtime](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/7d0e4d7bad7f602c63af01526d8b4d316e05e32e/certbot/main.py#L741) largely by just parsing the command line arguments. For testing, it'd be nice to have a config with all the default values (except a couple which may be modified to point to things like temporary directories). The reason this is nice is many tests duplicate the work of setting up these values so the tests run properly. If they don't, code may fail due to an attribute error or being unexpectedly passed a `mock` object somewhere down the line. Some tests may changes a value or two to not be the default, but the test changing it's copy of the config shouldn't be a problem and allowing the test to focus on what it's doing differently rather than defining boilerplate makes both reading and writing tests easier. #4435 which I just added more information to describes a way to create this default config. While I'd take a PR that duplicates the keys and values in the config here, it'd be cleaner and more maintainable in the long run to use the approach specified in #4435 to help solve this PR.

Happy to answer any questions if you have them.

Do you by chance have multiple virtual hosts in a single file, especially the HTTP/HTTPS ones for the domains that Certbot is obtaining certificates for? While doing this is completely valid in Apache, Certbot doesn't properly support it right now. Unfortunately, previous versions of Certbot would often try to parse multiple vhosts in the same file, only sometimes doing so correctly. We've fixed this and Certbot currently ignores vhosts that it's not expected it can parse, but this change has caused the problems you're seeing for other users. We expect Certbot will have real support for this in the next release at the beginning of next month though and I can confirm that using `webroot` will fix the problem.

Hi @TitanChris.  Using `--test-cert` / `--staging` flag will produce non-valid certs (they're issued by a staging version of the Let's Encrypt API).  It will never overwrite existing valid certs (unless you provide `--break-my-certs`).  What you probably want is that flag, with the `webroot` plugin.

There is also a `--dry-run` flag which implies `--staging`, but tries to basically test all the steps of obtaining a cert without actually writing it to disk.  It only supports `certonly` at the moment, but we should add support for `run` too.

This is a weird case. This is how I (somehow) reproduced it:


## The test code

```
# cat anno.py
import subprocess
import unittest

from pyannotate_runtime import collect_types
from schematics import Model
from schematics.types import StringType


class GitPackageSource(Model):
    uri = StringType(required=True)
    tag = StringType(serialize_when_none=False)
    branch = StringType(serialize_when_none=False)
    commit = StringType(serialize_when_none=False)


class SourceProcessor(object):
    def sleep(self):
        subprocess.check_output(["sleep", "1"])
        return "ok"


class TestUtil(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_SourceProcessor_helpers(self):
        sp = SourceProcessor()

        for i in [{"uri": "git@foo"}, {"tag": "foo"}, {"commit": "1234"}]:
            g = GitPackageSource(i)
        print("GitPackageSource instantiated")

        self.assertEqual(sp.sleep(), "ok")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    collect_types.init_types_collection()
    with collect_types.collect():
        unittest.main()
```

## The setup process

```
{~}mkdir test && cd test
{~/test}virtualenv venv
New python executable in /Users/shi.kaix/test/venv/bin/python2.7
Also creating executable in /Users/shi.kaix/test/venv/bin/python
Installing setuptools, pip, wheel...done.
{~/test}source venv/bin/activate
∇{~/test}pip install pyannotate schematics
Successfully installed mypy-extensions-0.3.0 pyannotate-1.0.6 schematics-2.1.0 six-1.11.0 typing-3.6.4
∇{~/test}for i in `seq 1 10`; do gtimeout 5 python anno.py; echo $?; done
```

That is, create a new directory, init a virtualenv, install some dependencies and run the test code 10 times. I've observed an 80% chance that the test code will not finish within 5 seconds.

## The output

A typical output could look like this:

```
GitPackageSource instantiated
124
GitPackageSource instantiated
124
GitPackageSource instantiated
124
GitPackageSource instantiated
124
GitPackageSource instantiated
124
GitPackageSource instantiated
.
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 1.005s

OK
0
GitPackageSource instantiated
124
GitPackageSource instantiated
124
GitPackageSource instantiated
124
GitPackageSource instantiated
.
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 1.134s

OK
0
```

So most of the time, the `gtimeout` command exit with `124`, which signify that the python process has been killed.


## My notes

1. The original command that I've used is `git ls-remote git@github.com:cloudtools/stacker.git`, which would give some output. It took around 1 second to finish on my end and the error rate is around 80%. I switched it to `sleep 1` and it give nearly the same error rate.
2. You will of course note that there is a `GitPackageSource instantiated` message there in the output. We can see that happen all the time and it always finished successfully. However, if we remove this part:

```
        for i in [{"uri": "git@foo"}, {"tag": "foo"}, {"commit": "1234"}]:
            g = GitPackageSource(i)
        print("GitPackageSource instantiated")
```

The error rate would drop significantly to around 10%. If we replace `GitPackageSource` with `UserDict`, the error rate would be around 10%.

3. If we instead of sending `SIGTERM` to the python process and instruct `gtimeout` to send `SIGINT` to the process, we'll get:

trait
A TraitType or a tuple of TraitTypes with length 2. In the case of a TraitType, the trait will validate a dict's values. In the case of a tuple the first element validates keys and the second validates values - Dict(traits=(Unicode(), Int()).
trait_mapping
A dict of traits keyed on values (identical to per_key_traits). If we really wanted to make things backward compatible, then we could just keep the name traits instead of trait_mapping.

It is certainly by design - the original motivating example was the `.ssh` folder storing your private ssh keys. Refusing to serve files from hidden folders makes it a bit harder for potential attackers to get that information or to e.g. add themself to the authorized keys for ssh-ing in. It's still possible through running a kernel, but there are some classes of attack where HTTP requests would be possible but controlling a kernel would not. This is defence in depth: if an outer layer of security is imperfect, it limits what someone can do.

However, I think we could relax it a bit. In particular, if you've launched the notebook from a hidden folder, you probably want to serve that folder (possibly you don't think of it as hidden). So we could refuse to serve hidden *subfolders* within the starting directory, but not check the starting directory itself.

For the record, part of the reason we haven't been paying attention to this is that while *Anaconda* has been getting a lot of issues filed about this and linking to this issue, this issue itself has only been getting a couple of comments a month. I don't get notifications from Anaconda issues, so I didn't see all those related issues until I came to this page. That's not anyone's fault, it's just one of those things that happens.

Perhaps your interrupts are only killing the parent, rather than the server itself. The Jupyter Notebook is only one process (not counting kernels, but those won't hold the server's port), so if anything exits it's the notebook server.

After looking at the code: the problem is in notebook/base/handlers.py

def clear_login_cookie(self):
        cookie_options = self.settings.get('cookie_options', {})
        path = cookie_options.setdefault('path', self.base_url)
        **self.clear_cookie(self.cookie_name, path=path) // first clear_cookie**
        if path and path != '/':
            # also clear cookie on / to ensure old cookies
            # are cleared after the change in path behavior.
            **self.clear_cookie(self.cookie_name) // second clear_cookie**

The problem is in the last line where I've marked //second clear_cookie. Commenting this out fixes the problem.

The issue is that clear_cookie is implemented by the Tornado backend on the server side by marking the cookie as expired. Cookies don’t actually expire on the server though, they are implemented by the client browser on the http response. Cookies have paths, the first call to clear_cookie on the line where I marked // first clear_cookie has a path set correctly, and the Tornado backend sets the cookie for expiration. The second call to clear_cookie inside the if block has no path set, so the Tornado backend gets confused and messes up the cookie expiration from the first call. So when the browser gets the response, it doesn’t clear the cookie.

The reason this doesn’t happen if Jupyter doesn’t have a base_url is because in that case path=’’, so the two clear_cookie calls are equivalent.

The easiest fix it to comment out the second call to clear_cookie, if we don't care about accounting for the behavior of cookies from old Jupyter versions. I'm not sure what a full fix is yet.

This is in some ways really a Tornado bug with dealing with cookies with paths rather than a Jupyter bug.

Oh jeez, here we go again.

This topic has been discussed extensively (internally) regarding Rugged and the transition to Ruby 1.9.2, which as you know, also adds support for encodings on strings.

Basically, what we agreed upon is letting the user choose, on a per-repository basis, on which locale are the strings (paths) for that repository encoding. We default this to UTF-8, but the user is able to change the encoding for any given repo as soon as he notices transcodings starting to fail.

The library automatically tags all the strings coming out from the repo with such encoding. This is not the most elegant solution, but it's the only one that works in 100% of the cases: if the user is not able to manually specify an encoding, there is no reliable way whatsoever to detect it. And I prefer to keep guessing to a minimum.

Regarding Ruby 1.8.7, we just return raw bytes as strings, since this version of Ruby doesn't have proper unicode support. I understand that the smart thing to do is returning Unicode strings in Python 2.X too, since otherwise the API would break between version.

Anyway, this is what we're rolling with for Rugged. Just thought I'd let you know.

The error is caused because of our YAML parser. Because of the internal representation of the YAML structure, a YAML document can only contain one structure at the top level (e.g. a YAML list, or a YAML map). Comments are then stored as nodes in the internal YAML parser representation (just like lists, maps, etc), so that means there can not be a comment and then a map at the top level. Please be aware that for this reason SOPS will not preserve comments on the top of the YAML file, even if this panic was fixed. The panic can definitely be fixed, but the comment is not going to be preserved any time soon as it'd require heavy modification to the parser.

I think you might want to study about the chunked encoding a bit more. When chunked encoding is present, content-length header does not make any sense and it is ignored. If the transfer encoding is not chunked then content-length header is required to determine the length of the payload. If neither is present the closing connection indicates to the client of the end of the entity. [Read more about message length calculation in RFC 2616](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-4.4).

So I also learned that RandomCrop when you set `pad_if_needed` to True does not RandomPad. AKA, whereas random crop applies a crop at a random offset to the image, `pad_if_needed` performs a centered padding, which does not randomly place the image in the padded image. The goal for padding should be that the image is in a random spot. Imagine the following:

Imagine the image, whatever size, on an infinite canvas.
Apply a random offset, and a random crop, to that canvas, such that:
if the crop is bigger than the original image, at least some of the original image remains in the output.
if the crop is smaller than the original image, all of the output is part of the original image.

Here is a minimal example that shows why. In this case, the `SHARED_VALUE` variable would represent the root logger. 3 files:

In parent.py:

```python
import luigi
from shared_variable import set_shared_value
from child import ChildTask

# Same functionality as setting up a logger:
set_shared_value('foo')

class ParentTask(luigi.WrapperTask):
    def requires(self):
        for i in range(0,4):
            yield ChildTask(item=str(i))
```

In child.py:

```python
import luigi
from shared_variable import get_shared_value

class ChildTask(luigi.Task):
    
    item = luigi.Parameter()

    def output(self):
        return luigi.LocalTarget("output" + str(self.item))

    def run(self):
        value = get_shared_value()
        with open("output" + str(self.item), "w") as file:
            file.write(value) 
```

In shared_variable.py

```python
SHARED_VALUE = None

def set_shared_value(value):
  global SHARED_VALUE
  SHARED_VALUE = value

def get_shared_value():
  global SHARED_VALUE
  if SHARED_VALUE is None:
    raise Exception('SHARED_VALUE has not been set, please run set_shared_value() first.')
  return SHARED_VALUE
```

I think there is an problem in the `weak_cross_entropy_2d` [right here](https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/65e1a791e1c3780de827a0704947343ad61953a1/tflearn/objectives.py#L186) where the [get_shape](https://www.tensorflow.org/versions/r0.11/api_docs/python/framework.html#Tensor.get_shape) returns `TensorShape` with last element being `Dimension(1)`
In my opinion this line should be `num_classes = y_true.get_shape().as_list()[-1]`

A workaround is to set the `num_classes` directly by creating a wrapper of `weak_cross_entropy_2d` ([see the relevant discussion](https://github.com/MarvinTeichmann/tensorflow-fcn/issues/1#issuecomment-224822000))
```python
def weak_cross_entropy_2d_wrapper(y_pred, y_true):
    return weak_cross_entropy_2d(y_pred, y_true, num_classes=1)
```
and using 
```python
network = regression(network, 
                     optimizer='adam',
                     loss=weak_cross_entropy_2d_wrapper,

```
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import os
pyltp_root="/home/xxx/work/language/tools/pyltp"
from pyltp import Segmentor
segmentor = Segmentor()  # 初始化实例
segmentor.load(os.path.join(pyltp_root,"ltp_data/cws.model"))  # 加载模型
words = segmentor.segment("小明来到北京大学")  # 分词
print '\ '.join(words)
segmentor.release()  # 释放模型

from pyltp import Postagger
postagger = Postagger() # 初始化实例
postagger.load(os.path.join(pyltp_root,"ltp_data/pos.model"))  # 加载模型
postags = postagger.postag(words)  # 词性标注
print '\t'.join(postags)
postagger.release()  # 释放模型

from pyltp import NamedEntityRecognizer
recognizer = NamedEntityRecognizer() # 初始化实例
recognizer.load(os.path.join(pyltp_root,"ltp_data/ner.model"))  # 加载模型
netags = recognizer.recognize(words, postags)  # 命名实体识别
print '\t'.join(netags)
recognizer.release()  # 释放模型

from pyltp import Parser
parser = Parser() # 初始化实例
parser.load(os.path.join(pyltp_root,"ltp_data/parser.model"))  # 加载模型
arcs = parser.parse(words, postags)  # 句法分析
print "\t".join("%d:%s" % (arc.head, arc.relation) for arc in arcs)
parser.release()  # 释放模型

from pyltp import SementicRoleLabeller
labeller = SementicRoleLabeller() # 初始化实例
labeller.load(os.path.join(pyltp_root,"ltp_data/srl/srl.model"))  # 加载模型
roles = labeller.label(words, postags, netags, arcs)  # 语义角色标注
for role in roles:
    print role.index, "".join(
        ["%s:(%d,%d)" % (arg.name, arg.range.start, arg.range.end) for arg in role.arguments])
labeller.release()  # 释放模型

```

@cheshire That may well be, but we're not the people teaching Java programmers how to write programs. We have to make sure Z3 is minimally painful to use, and in this case that means we have to collect Context objects. And obviously, that is what we used to do and so many of our users are still used to it (and nobody told them they have to change their programs either; they don't read our commit messages).

The issue is that the QF_ABV logic does not include the function "const". 
You can use Z3 without specifying a logic. In this case, the parser's symbol table includes the "const" function. For SMT-LIB2 logics, it will exclude "const" from being a known symbol.

All this business is a huge mess and I'm inclined not to support anything but the most basic default-installation. 

The `--prefix` option is definitely shown in `--help` on every platform except Windows. If it didn't show for you and if os.name returns `nt`, the problem is that you're using a Windows-Python in the MSYS+friends shells. The right versions return `MSYS_NT` or `MINGW` (which the scripts detect correctly). 

Clearly, we can't support the cross product of Pythons and MSYS/MinGW setups, the user will just have to run the "right" Python. We could try to emit warning messages when we detect some non-trivial combination of tools, but I'm not sure it's worth the effort.

Yes, that's correct. The problem is that s.to_smt2() uses a mixture of SMT version 1.0 and 2.0 and floats never existed in 1.0 times. This is fixed now, but the converter will always print decimal float-literals instead of bit-vector based `(fp #...)` literals. We'll have to do some more refactoring until all options are available in all printers, etc.

That may be true for a pure Python project like the Z3 Python API, but that's only a small fraction of Z3. For instance, some users will want to use two or more APIs and perhaps the binary as well, and we definitely don't want to publish packages for the cross product of all configurations. 

An example of another native/binary code API is [llvmlite](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/llvmlite/0.8.0) and they recommend "Under a recent Ubuntu or Debian system, you may install the ...system package... if available." (and similar for other OSs). Are there any popular examples of this type of PyPI package? I also found [pySMT](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pySMT/0.4.2) which also relies on system installations of solvers and their APIs. 

In my opinion, the best place for setup.py would be in src/api/python and it will install only the API bindings, but not libz3.dll/.so/.dylib/.exe and we should rely on a system-wide installation of Z3. We have the same issue with the OCaml API, see issue #34. Whatever we do, we should at the very least make sure that we follow the same strategy for all APIs. It's fine if we print "you now need to install the system Z3 package" at the end of the installation if we can't find libz3.dll/.so/.dylib in the path.

For lex combinations of MaxSAT Z3 solves one problem at a time. It commits the last state from each round, which forces any solution to later round maxsat problems to have the same values for the previous rounds. This approach amounts to the one that shows better performance in http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007%2Fs10472-011-9233-2.
 
I now tuned the code for the special case of multiple maxsat problems, 
so performance may have improved (the default code path introduces push/pop operations, which increases clause sizes uniformly). Otherwise, a sanity check in terms of a trace from your scenario may provide additional clues about how to tune.

then during same loop iteration, loop wakes up handler task because it has something to write. 
2. transport get closed, web_protocol got connection_lost and cancel handler tasks,
    but handler task will get cancel exception during next loop iteration
3. handler get called and started to write to closed transport.

that is the reason why RuntimeError is not good idea in async transport.

Performing the "gets" from a database would be done by using the database object's `__getitem()__` and `__iter()__` functions respectively.  Both of these will return a `Document` or a `DesignDocument` when appropriate.  For example:

```python
doc = database['my_doc']  #returns a Document
ddoc = database['_design/my_ddoc'] # returns a DesignDocument

# iterate through documents retrieved via _all_docs endpoint
for doc in database:
    if isinstance(doc, DesignDocument):
        print '{0} is a design doc'.format(doc['_id'])
    else:
        print '{0} is a document'.format(doc['_id'])
```

However, when creating a document it does not get "promoted" to a `DesignDocument` if the id provided starts with `_design/`.  IMO this should be changed so that the promotion does takes place.  I have an FB case for this particular issue already.

One thing of note is that obviously with Python you don't have to type your variable as you would in Java or similar language.  So for instance in the case of the iteration above, `doc` has no issue switching from `Document` to `DesignDocument` and the user can pretty much remain oblivious to that and treat each `doc` as a `Document` if they wish.  But the option is there if they want it to treat the `doc` as a `DesignDocument` if they so choose and of course it actually is a `DesignDocument`.

This happens because of the way that the dates are inferred from the tokens. It interprets the first 3 numbers as Year, Month, Date, then it has a bunch of 2-digit numeric tokens left. The logic is that if it finds a 2-digit numeric token *after* the year, month and date are determined, then that is an hour. As a result, each subsequent 2-digit number it finds just replaces the existing hour.

PR #32 fixes this, though it does so just by making sure that we've already found an hour, we won't continue to interpret this as the correct hour. Again, I'm thinking that this should be addressed in a more fundamental way when Issues #19 and #28 are addressed.

First the VQ-VAE is trained (as in this notebook). This allows you to compress images into discrete codes with the encoder, and reconstruct with the decoder. If you want to sample new images you can train a generative model (e.g., PixelCNN) on the discrete codes (instead of on the pixels). Once the PixelCNN is trained you take the generated codes and use the decoder network to get the image. This is what is meant with a **prior**, as in the VAE sense: a model on top of the latents. So in the paper the PixelCNN is **not** used as a decoder (but that's also possible).

This is not a `dronekit-sitl` specific problem.
The cause of your problem is [System Integrity Protection](https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204899), a macOS feature which write-protects `/usr`, `/bin`, `/sbin`, `/System` (not even root can edit or add files in those directories).

So, you cannot install packages in the macOS-provided Python installation in a system-wide fashion.

You have two alternatives:

1. Use a **virtual environment**. You should almost always install packages in a virtual environment in order not to pollute your system-wide Python installation. I recommend the use of [pipenv](https://docs.pipenv.org/), the [officially recommended](https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/managing-dependencies/#managing-dependencies) python packaging tool.
2. **Install a new Python distribution in a writable location.** You can do that easily with [Homebrew](https://brew.sh/), by typing `brew install python2`.
Probably you should do this even if you're following solution n. 1, since you will need to install/upgrade `pip`, `pipenv`, `setuptools`, etc. system-wide.

Alternatively, you may disable SIP, but this is a very bad idea since it would expose you to additional security threats.

Hey guys, so after some more messing around I solved the errors. 

1) The ABI must be is list format, not string format! 
2) The ABI of a contract actually is a list containing different dictionaries inside. You will need to see which ABI corresponds to your event based on 'name' and 'type' values in the ABI. 
3) Once you identify which event you are looking for you use the same code but only pass the abi specific to this event. Any other events might fail in parsing but all the correct events should be correctly parsed and data returned. 

ABI example
```
contract_abi = While the keypresses are nice for the standalone viewer, I think we should think carefully about how best define what keypresses / mouse events do in the context of a Glue viewer. So for example, toggling color maps should probably be done via a toolbar dropdown like the image viewer.

Second, the ginga project does a nice job about showing on-screen instructions for what the keyboard and mouse do, depending on the current interaction mode.

Hello,

Thanks for the report! What version did you upgrade from/to, and have you recently changed the version of Python you use with this library? The error you're reporting is one we had seen commonly around the time we dropped support for Python versions below 2.7.9 for security reasons--earlier versions of Python did not perform SSL certificate verification, which left users on those versions vulnerable.

Of course, normally you don't need to do anything special for certificate verification to just work. We've noticed in previously reported issues that some platforms "forget" the certificate directory; in these cases, it was typically resolved with a system restart and/or reinstallation of Python. Other times, this resulted because of the user's proxy settings.

If necessary, you can specify the [cafile](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads.yaml#L109) to use in the configuration file. As a last resort for a short-term fix, you could [disable certificate verification](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads.yaml#L110), but this is not recommended because of the aforementioned security vulnerability.

Regards,
Mark

Extra operators:
- [ ] first
- [ ] last
- [ ] head(size)
- [ ] tail(size)

Splitting the whole parent expression on the first occurrence of the parent expression’s operator will obviously not work:

```
(a * b + c) * d
^^^ ~~~~~~~~~~~
lhs         rhs

 b + c) * d*(a 
```

What can work is take the range between the end of `lhs` and start of `rhs` and `re.fullmatch` that against `r'(.*)\s*{}\s*(.*)'.format(re.escape(parent.op))`, and then adjust the range of lhs to (start of parent, end of lhs + len(match[1])) and then range of rhs to (start of rhs - len(match[2]), end of parent).

```
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  parent
(a * b + c) * d
 ^^^^^^^^^    ^
 lhs        rhs  as reported by asttokens

          ~~~~   fullmatch against (.*)\s*\*\s*(.*)
(a * b + c) * d
          ^      match[1]  (match[2] is empty)
^^^^^^^^^^^   ^
lhs         rhs  adjusted
```

Not the most elegant way, but it will do for now.

The other way to address is that doesn't involve modifying pyral is to use the project=None keyword arg in the call to rally.post (aka update).  Since FormattedID values span an entire workspace, if you know the FormattedID, there can only be a single instance of an artifact with that value in the workspace.  So, by specifying that project=None, if the artifact exists, it will be found and the rest of the post method works as designed.  Of course, if you prefer the local modification continue to use that.

Thanks for the PR, Meng.

I'm wondering about a different approach and would like your input.

This issue is really about how we represent the notion of an unknown protein effect. Your PR does this with special values for `posedit.pos` and `posedit.edit`. As an alternative, we could use var.posedit = None to indicate unknown effect.

Throughout the code, `pos` and `edit` are typically represented with class instances. We use None in some cases, which seems reasonable. My concern with the existing code is that we use some special case scalar values for `pos` and `edit` in places. For example, we use `edit == "0"` to indicate "no protein" (https://github.com/biocommons/hgvs/blob/master/hgvs/utils/altseq_to_hgvsp.py#L318).

Your PR continues the use of special values and is consistent with current code. 

However, I wonder whether we should move away from special values and toward explicit representation of these states.

As a practical example of the significance, suppose a user wants to know whether the protein effect is uncertain. With this PR, they would need to reimplement the logic regarding `pos` and `edit`. With the proposed alternative, they would use `var.posedit is None`, which I think is more straightforward.

I think two options exist:
* Update this PR to include a method like "protein_effect_unknown" which centralizes the logic
* Use `posedit = None` to represent unknown effect.

What do you think?

Another aspect that might be worth mentioned (or do we already): the main use case of `txaio` is **library authors**, _not_ **application developers**. Again, the reason is, that due to the (technically unavoidable) restrictions (under the goal of source compat.), and regular app developers will be disappointed likely.

The latest shit in the Python community is `async` / `await` - which, granted, is nice syntax - but Python 3.5+ only. So, I'd say, we should clearly point app developers to Python 3.5+ .. and scare away from using `txaio` directly.

Of course, Autobahn and Crossbar.io have different goals / requirements:

1. Autobahn absolutely must run on various combinations of Python version and network framework. And hence, we bite the bullet and refrain from using co-routine style altogether (within the library)
2. Crossbar.io is Twisted only, and hence _can_ use co-routines, because the syntax for Twisted co-routines is the same for Python 2.7 and Python 3.3+ (including 3.5+ - which are the Python versions Crossbar.io can run on top)

> I also think, above also hints at the following: we probably should discourage writing Python app components to be run in Crossbar.io side-by-side (embedded in router workers) or container workers. Because that will essentially impose Twisted. Instead, we should recommend writing (new) Python components in Python 3.5+ (in asyncio) and running as guest workers. However, this is a separate issue - and would need discussion ...
(BTW, thanks for tracking this down and for a clear bug report!)

A word of caution. A repo containing lots of jobs with the Dirac backend will take a long time to progress between states. This is related to a combination of factors:

1. Large repos take a long time (still) to load into memory for monitoring. I plan to look at this further as time goes on.
2. Lots of jobs on Dirac require lots of forwards and backwards communication which happens on sub-threads currently making profiling/debugging more difficult that it should be.
3. If a single job causes problems the Montoring loop will get stuck (or delayed) for all jobs using that backend. This is probably related to the issue at hand but is a pain when the backend isn't the simple case of running on the Localhost.
4. Communication with Dirac often gets held up when one command suddenly takes much longer than usual to complete. It's not known if this is due to Ganga or the way we're accessing Dirac atm.

These jobs upload the data to EOS and then move it to a different location in EOS by copying it to the client first and then uploading it again. Merger jobs would suffer from similar issues. The **templates** that we have put in the framework for but in reality never developed would be a nice way to demonstrate jobs that could do some of these operations (like merging DiracFiles).

I need to check. This is something I hadn't thought about at all so far but I should. I agree that features.fea inside UFO should be self-contained. Ideally, the kerning classes should be kept in groups.plist only if the FEA does not contain "feature kern" at all, but if it does, then they should be included. 

Karsten Luecke's [KLTF Make Kern Feature](http://kltf.de/kltf_otproduction.shtml) macro writes its kerning classes **inside** the "feature kern" block rather than into the prolog, which I actually like a lot (I never thought this was possible), so the entire "feature kern" block is self-contained, with class definitions, subtable breaks and the pos statements. This is how it should be done -- put non-kerning classes (used in sub statements) in the prolog but put kerning class definitions inside the "feature kern" block if it is being written into FEA.

With this change the static message pool can be completely removed from OpenThread in favor of a platform defined pool. The variable counter of free messages is no longer passed from OpenThread to the platform.

The primary motivation for this is to better facilitate NCP referencing both OpenThread and the Radio layer. Since NCP always has an OT instance, it would require OpenThread to have the radio disabled when the interface is down, so that NCP could use it.

From my experience with other wireless devices, such as Wi-Fi, while the sleep state does save most power it is still not the same as disabled. On Windows, when a device is plugged in, we will always have an `otInstance` initialized, but don't always want the radio on necessarily.

Thanks for the feedback @kingosticks. I've been thinking about this some more and can perhaps provide additional context below.

>> Dealing with large tracklists is problematic on the client side, which feels like something that could probably benefit from being able to use generators.

> I don't follow. Are you talking about python generators here? For pagination? Problematic, as in slow?

Yes to all of the above. One could argue that having thousands of tracks in a single list is not a valid use case, but it would be nice if MMW could handle this more elegantly. Chrome seems to do better than the other browsers at dealing with this. Firefox eventually displays a popup warning message that the script is taking longer than expected, which can be a little jarring for the user.

>> Although one could easily go overboard, having access to everything on PyPI could make it easier to implement things like caching and retrieving cover art and album info from other sources.

> Between Mopidy providing coverart and what we get from last.fm - are we not sorted in this department? This seems hypothetical.

Yes I will concede this is hypothetical. Caching is the one area that we might want to look into, as well as reducing the number of track lists that we keep in memory (MMW pretty much copies the same list to every pane at this stage). I think this will be easier to rectify server-side.

>> There are some hard-coded configuration features that should ideally be moved out to mopidy.conf

> Is any of that useful for users to configure? The less configuration options available the better.

Because MMW is a 'general purpose' frontend, users have the freedom to plug in any backend that they want and expect MMW to be able to serve it up. In the past MMW made assumptions about which back ends are out there, what they should be called, and if they can be searched. This might not work for everybody all of the time.

The way MMW works at the moment, users should be able to configure human-friendly names to display on-screen at the very least.

>> It might be useful to have access to Python's introspection features to figure out if a backend for example has searching implemented.

> I think this is something that should be provided to us by the Mopidy API. But I don't think there is anything planned in this department. We should look at proposing it.

Agreed. To be realistic however, the Mopidy issue list is almost 200 items deep and many of those will have a higher priority than this. I know PRs are welcomed but frankly struggle to keep up with and contribute to the core in addition to working on multiple extensions. Perhaps we just need an interim solution until we can come up with a generic approach for doing extension feature detection in the core?

> My big concern is that any extra interface creates an additional point of failure. Keeping an eye on things is much easier said than done. And while the Mopidy API is great at remaining compatible between versions, I'm not sure how solid something like this would be. And then we have our own backwards compatibility problems to think about. There is something to be said for just consuming what we are given.

Yes I think it comes down to just deciding if we should leave good enough alone. I personally think we are fairly safe in relying on the Mopidy.js approach. If it ever broke, then *none* of the HTTP clients would work anymore. But I could be wrong.

> Saying all that, I do see merit in being able to do more server-side things and the websocket events are nice to work with. Events aside though, you can probably make a more conventional templating approach quite neat. I'm just not sold on this.

I like the idea of just using templates but am not sure how to apply this in MMW:

- we're already using web sockets and promises for Mopidy models, and will now introduce another template-based approach for MMW models.
- part of the reason MMW feels so responsive is because it loads most of what it needs into index.html when it starts up, and then just dynamically shows or hides sections using jQuery panes. Tornado organizes apps using request handlers and templates. If we just have one big handler for index.html then we are back to not being able to modularize our code properly, and end up with one massive dictionary for all content. If we organize it properly into different URL's, handlers, and templates, then every user action will trigger a request / response cycle which will probably slow things down a bit.

So I am unsure where to go from here. Perhaps we just leave it as is and not embark on moving stuff to the server side. There are some other useful elements in this PR that I can strip out and resubmit.

I spent a little time trying to think of a good way to handle this, as well as #254 (ability for hooks to target types of objects, e.g. file/symlink/submodule) and #220 (ability for hooks to target "text" files and avoid list of "text" extensions).

Here's my suggestion:

#### Files get automatically tagged

Each file gets tagged with a list of tags describing it. For example, an executable file `something.py` might be classified `['file', 'text', 'python', 'executable']`.

#### Hooks target file types

Hooks can then specify `types` (which defaults to `file`):

```
# hooks.yaml
-   id: trailing-whitespace
    types: [text]
    entry: trailing-whitespace-fixer

-   id: symlinks-point-inside-repo
    types: [symlink]

-   id: executables-have-shebang
    types: [executable]

-   id: double-quote-string-fixer
    types: [python, ruby, javascript]  # if this hook actually worked on all of these
```

There's an added benefit in that we no longer need to duplicate the hook extensions in repos if we want to change the path.

```
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
-   id: puppet-lint
    path: ^modules/ocf
```

Currently we have to do `exclude: ^modules/(?!ocf).*$` instead which will get really complex as soon as we need to start excluding anything else. Now instead of hooks targeting files by extension, the suggested way is targeting them by type. (You can still do it by extension, of course.)

`types` is an OR operation; a file with any of the tags will match.

I would suggest not including an `all` types alias, since I think it risks people copy-pasting it without realizing it includes symlinks and submodules. `[file, symlink, submodule]` is not very cumbersome for the very few hooks that will actually want it (the only existing one I can think of is `check-case-conflict`).


#### Implementation

Making this happen is fairly easy (and I'd volunteer to do the work, since I really want to check files with shebangs):

* Write a file classifier. A very functional classifier is easy to write (check if text, then check extension, fallback to reading shebang) and covers almost all cases.

* A little plumbing

It's pretty much backwards compatible. Nothing changes about `path` or `exclude`. If omitted, `types` defaults to `['file']` (technically a breaking change, but I think it's a much better default (many of the current hooks will follow symlinks outside of the repo and do nasty things). I can't see any cases where we would break existing stuff.

The main issue is if people upgrade hooks but don't upgrade pre-commit. I don't know a good solution here, unfortunately.

> system hooks defeat the purpose of pre-commit. The point is to isolate hook executables from the user's environment to allow for repeatable, no-installation execution.

Inside create_factor_tear_sheet the following functions plotting.plot_quantile_returns_bar, plotting.plot_quantile_returns_violin and plotting.plot_cumulative_returns_by_quantile are called with returns calculated via perf.mean_return_by_quantile.

perf.mean_return_by_quantile calls utils.demean_forward_returns (the function that actually performs the "demean") and there is no way for the user to avoid that behaviour. So they end up looking at biased returns if they intend to run a long only portfolio.

Also, the function plotting.plot_cumulative_returns is called with returns calculated via perf.factor_returns. The latter function has a concept of long/short or long only portfolio (long_short argument), but there is no option for the user that simply calls create_factor_tear_sheet to switch that option.

You can pass `strict=False` to the constructor.

```python
class Person(Model):
    first_name = StringType()
    age = IntType()

Person({'x': ''})  # => raise ModelConversionError: {'x': 'Rogue field'}
Person({'x': ''}, strict=False)  # => no error
```

`import_data` also does not care about unrecognized data:

```python
Person().import_data({'x': ''})  # => No error
```

Subclassing is very fragile, it's easy to override something unintentionally or have a namespace conflict between your attributes and the attributes of the parent class. Since the parent class can add or remove attributes over time, this is just unmaintainable and not easy to port between versions.

The best way to implement this feature is with composition, where the "type encoding strategy" is an argument to the encoder. Since there is only one thing to override, the strategy is simply a callable rather than a class with some methods.

Switching to a default method is faster because simplejson can do caching of instances when certain arguments are not overridden.

If there was a library available with exactly the V8 algorithm to format floats then we could add an option here to use it, but implementing such a thing is not within the scope of this project (especially considering that it would also require a pure Python implementation of that algorithm).

As an image generation researcher, I require the ability to display images at actual size for images up to 1024x1024. Viewing 1024x1024 images scaled down to 1000x1000 would introduce scaling artifacts and I would not be able to see which artifacts come from my image generation model and which come from TensorBoard.

I would prefer to have 2000x2000 images displayed at 2000x2000. If it's necessary to show them within a smaller card, I would want the "show actual size" button to cause them to be displayed at actual size and activate scrolling within the card, similar to how viewing images at actual size in Photoshop/Gimp/eog works. But if something weird happens for images larger than 1024x1024, I can work around that by always chopping my images into chunks of size 1024x1024.

```python
x = index_of_current_event()

   # but probably better than allowing an unrealistically large interval

```

In my opinion, it is notably easier to distinguish the colors than in the palette with reduced opacity: for instance: color pairs {1,3} and {5,6} and {6,7}. The reduced opacity is precisely the problem with the current palette, not so much the hues! so reintroducing it doesn't make much sense, in my opinion.

Yes, I echo the OP's thoughts exactly; as a designer, it is unnerving when I see the "not symbol" used incorrectly. As the OP stated, the intention of the design of the symbol's slash being drawn from top-left to bottom-right is to invoke a negative declaration when reading from left-to-right. The "motion" is therefore in the downward direction. This symbol was specifically designed to invoke such subconscious emotions to the reader, i.e., down = negative, red = negative/stop/danger/loss, up = positive, green = positive/gain/go/proceed/ok, etc.

I used this profiling code to do some timing tests.  Across several runs there is little difference between using the id(obj) approach and using time.time 

```
import music21 as m21
from uuid import uuid4
import cProfile, pstats, io
import time

def test_1():
    for x in range(0,10000):
        anote = m21.note.Note('C', id='d-'+str(uuid4()))

def test_2():
    for x in range(0,10000):
        anote = m21.note.Note('C')

def test_3():
    for x in range(0,10000):
        anote = m21.note.Note('C', id=str(time.time()))

if __name__ == "__main__":

    sortby = 'cumulative'

    pr1 = cProfile.Profile()
    pr1.enable()
    print('With UUID')
    test_1()
    pr1.disable()
    s1 = io.StringIO()
    ps1 = pstats.Stats(pr1, stream=s1).sort_stats(sortby)
    ps1.print_stats()
    print(s1.getvalue())

    pr2 = cProfile.Profile()
    pr2.enable()
    print('With id(obj)')
    test_2()
    pr2.disable()
    s2 = io.StringIO()
    ps2 = pstats.Stats(pr2, stream=s2).sort_stats(sortby)
    ps2.print_stats()
    print(s2.getvalue())

    pr3 = cProfile.Profile()
    pr3.enable()
    print('With time.time')
    test_2()
    pr3.disable()
    s3 = io.StringIO()
    ps3 = pstats.Stats(pr3, stream=s3).sort_stats(sortby)
    ps3.print_stats()
    print(s3.getvalue())

```

I think we need to decide on some model for handling player. I think what @ids1024 is proposing about having a persistent player instance makes sense. That was a direction where I was going with proposing to drop everything else except mpv. 

Basically we could drop the hack that the player runs in background and receives keys and instead run persistent mpv process, control it using JSON IPC we are already using and handle all frontend in mps-youtube. This way we could truly manage playback state. This would simplify MPRIS (basically we would always have playback state available) and many other things with that.

I like that this PR is a first step from `if(mpv) else` hell we have now, but I'm not sure we need this extensible interface in the current form. In the best case all those `Player` subclasses will act as mpv replacement which does not bring any benefit by itself. In real scenario the other players will not have all features of mpv, because it will be impossible to implement them (as shown currently with vlc). This might bring new wave of bug reports for the kind of bugs we have from people that still use mplayer.

What I think we need is to support people that are "misusing" mps-youtube. The guy that used mps-youtube with mpd or the guy that used mps-youtube as backend for webpage (or web interface to mps-youtube?). This is (or was) a great advantage of current design where you can just stick your own command to config (but this is mostly broken now). I think for this kind of extensibility something like our current NOTIFIER api is better. An API where you can stick your own command or script and it gives you url to play for each song (and does not require anything from you). This is what I have meant by bring-your-own-player architecture, actually.

To elaborate a bit more on how a `Condition` could help here: a `Condition` always has an attached `Lock`. And the basic pattern of using a `Condition` is: one thread acquires the lock, checks some stuff, and then blocks in `Condition.wait` – which temporarily releases the lock. Another thread acquires the lock, mutates some stuff, and then calls `Condition.notify` – which can only be done with the lock held. So for example, we might write:

```python
# initialize
self._cond = threading.Condition()

# going to sleep
with self._cond:
    if self._stopped:
        return
    self._cond.wait()

# waking up
with self._cond:
    self._stopped = True
    self._cond.notify()
```

This is the same pattern as our current code, *except* that before we had a problem because the wake-up thread could run in between when the sleeping thread checks `self._stopped`, and when it calls `wait` to go to sleep. Now, the sleeping thread holds the lock for this period, so the wake-up thread can't run during this time. So that race condition is solved. Does that make sense?

My intuition says that we can extend this pretty straightforwardly to the slightly more complex full watchdog code, but right now I'm too sleepy to figure it out, so maybe give it a try and see how far you get?

For 2, each `ray.put` and `ray.get` conceptually consists of two parts: serialization/deserialization and copying to/from the object store. We combine them for performance reasons, but it may make sense to do the "serialization/deserialization" part on the `ExternalClient` and the "copy to/from the object store" on the `GateWay`. Could something like that work?

The serialization part is done with, e.g.,

```python
serialized_obj = ray.pyarrow.serialize(obj).to_buffer().to_pybytes()
ray.pyarrow.deserialize(serialized_obj)
```

The copy to/from the object store can be done with

```
object_id = ray.pyarrow.plasma.ObjectID(np.random.bytes(20))
buff = ray.worker.global_worker.plasma_client.create(object_id, len(serialized_obj))

# Do the copy. There's almost certainly a better way of doing this of course.
for i in range(len(serialized_obj)):
    buff[i] = serialized_obj[i]

ray.worker.global_worker.plasma_client.seal(object_id)

###### Get
ray.worker.global_worker.plasma_client.get_buffers([object_id])[0]
```

## Issue
The issue is we had redundancy in calls, inside `__delitem__, we called:
```python
        self._row_partitions = _map_partitions(
            del_helper, self._row_partitions, to_delete)
```
and then we called: 
```python
            self._col_partitions[i] = _deploy_func.remote(
                del_helper, self._col_partitions[i], to_delete_in_partition)
```
because we already deleted the data, the column deletion won't succeed. 

The implementation note in docstring states:
```
Notes: This operation happen on row and column partition
                  simultaneously. No rebuild.
```
but setting row_partitions and col_partitions automatically "rebuild" the block paritions via `_create_block_partitions`.

## Fix
The fix is simply
- Just perform the `__delitem__` in one of the row/col partition scheme
- Leave the col_metadata reset computation as-is. 

## Question
I would go with fixing it by removing the row_parititions schemes because:
- col_paritions should be generally faster and memory efficient than row_parititions because
        - `row_partitions` create intermediate "copy" of each row
        - `col_partitions`, with some tweaks, need only touch the part of columns that need to be deleted without memory overhead

Thanks for reaching out to us, I think it is a great idea to integrate alternate approaches or improvements, as you describe, into trackpy. There has been some recent issues that start to transition trackpy into a package a collection for particle tracking codes. This is a great opportunity to actually do it.

Point-by-point:

1. Background subtraction using an isotropic (Gaussian or disc-like) kernel seems to be a good idea. I guess we could make a toolbox of different preprocessing functions. We don't have a test for testing for this background-subtraction-overlap-bias. Recently I realized that this is certainly present in trackpy, because we choose this kernel size on the large side.
2. Although I did some literature research on this subject, the Newton tangent method is new to me. I think the refinement functions (center-of-mass, newton tangent, least squares fitting #350) each should have a different function, from which the user could choose. First we will have to make the tracking more modular (#257) to make it easy for users to roll their own selection of steps.
3. Deconvolution: I know many people use the commercial Huygens software to solve the deconvolution problem, how does this compare to your code?
4. Agreed for a long term goal!

>
> I've thought about this and bit, and my personal take on it is that if it is unlikely, or outside of our caring, that someone would only want to run a part of that 4-step GATK pre-processing, then it should be supplied to the user as "gatk_preprocessing", with RTC dynamically calling IR which dynamically calls BQSR which dynamically calls PrintReads which returns the processed BAM.

In my view of a "just world", no one ever runs INDEL realignment ever again (conditioned on them using a reassembly based variant caller --> e.g., HaplotypeCaller; you somatic people using IR + MuTect are still OK in my book). But yes, if what you're saying is that we don't package up the GATK as a "tool" (and we just package it as a pipeline), that is OK by me.

However, my point still stands. I'm not 100% sure about Kallisto, but other alignment-free quantifiers (thinking of Sailfish, for a sec) are two-pass (Index then Quantify). How do we accommodate those tools? Do we? Do we force them into a single Job?

The diff assumes that if you're in exclusive mode, you want to create. Which is a bit weird to me at first, but `open(2)` states
>In general, the behavior of O_EXCL is undefined if it is used without O_CREAT.  There is one exception: on Linux 2.6 and later, O_EXCL can be used without O_CREAT if pathname refers to a block device.  If the block device is in use by the system (e.g., mounted), open() fails with the error EBUSY.

So I think if you're using exclusive mode, then you're creating. Because you aren't using this library with paths to block devices. And also it's consistent with Python 3 where, if you `open()` with mode `x` and something else, instead of just `x` by itself, it will say `ValueError: must have exactly one of create/read/write/append mode`.

Ah yes, an EOF event will be pushed on the consumer queue as soon as the consumer reaches the current end-offset for a new offset, this is all done automatically in the background regardless if the app calls poll() or not.

If the produce rate is slow it is possible that we see something like this:
 * consumer reads msg 1, which is also EOF - emits EOF event
 * app calls poll() and sees msg 1 and EOF
 * producer produces msg 2
 * consumer reads msg 2 - which is also EOF - emits EOF event
 * producer produces msg 3
 * consumer reads msg 3, which is also EOF - emits EOF event
 * .. repeat producer + consumer cycle
 * app calls poll(), now sees 2, EOF, 3, EOF, 4, EOF, ..

The workaround is to set the poll timeout to 0 (immediate) when EOF is reached but continue polling until None (timeout) is returned.

The proposed patch changes the results for quite a lot of words in the Italian test vocabulary (in the snowball data module):

I don't really know Italian, so I can't easily judge if those changes are improvements (or at least harmless), or if this change results in the stemmer conflating cases which it shouldn't.

Note that overstemming (mapping two non-equivalent words the same output) is much more problematic than understemming (failing to map two equivalent words to the same output) for the intended domain of use of these stemmers (Information Retrieval).

The issue at hand here (at least from my perspective) is that snowball try to stem a stem (which is not necessarily a real German word).

Stems that are no real word should be left untouched.

I only had a quick glance so far, but two things I do not particulalry like about this PR: * a8e4d0e (Attempt to reuse results from findReferencedElements better): This seems to require a lot of assumptions on when the referencedIDs stay valid and when they have to be recalculated. While I immediately believe it can improve execution times I'm afraid it does not help overall code quality (e.g. less modular, harder to maintain, easier to introduce regressions in future, etc.).
I like the general idea of optimizing calls to findReferencedElements, but I'm not sure this is the correct approach. * c182fab (Rewrite findReferencedElements without recursion) 8c9c625 (Move loop inside a function to reduce the number of calls): As far as I understand this refactoring does not result in any measurable performance increase? If that's the case I'm not seeing the advantage as I feel the code becomes a bit more complicated for no gain... When I initially started working on Scour I liked the recursive implementation of many functions as it was elegant and easy to understand at the same time (and I still do). I believe the confusion is because there's two different ECDH keys at play.

One key is used by VAPID, and doesn't really figure into WebPush encryption. The other is used internally as part of the ECDH process. The comment you note points to the WebPush encryption ECDH key, which is indeed ephemeral for the given transaction. (ECDH is a fun way to melt your brain and convince yourself that math is broken.)

The ECDH used by VAPID is set by the DOM endpoint request. The public key is handed to the UserAgent, which can pass it to the Push Service in order to "lock" a given endpoint to a VAPID signature. You'll need to make sure that any future Subscription Updates are properly signed with the private half of the VAPID key. (See https://github.com/web-push-libs/vapid/tree/master/python  if you want an example library for doing that.)

VAPID is optional. (Heck, "Voluntary" is the "V" in VAPID) so WebPush doesn't really need to know anything about it.

I have fixed the initial issue I was having with Yocto which was due to libudev.h not being available in the build system.

I don't deny that having a self-contained build environment can be useful, however, for Linux this means you can only safely build a static library. To build a shared library (which is generally preferred) it has to be built against the libs installed by the distro's package manager or there is a high chance that the resulting librealsense library won't work when installed due to missing libs.

In my opinion there should be an option in the CMake script for building a shared library which ensures that pkgconfig is used to find all required dependencies (glfw, libusb etc...) and the library is built against these and fails if suitable versions are not found.

This looks good. One thing I'd be unsure about though is: when I have a mutation that returns a `DjangoObjectType`, is it the mutation database or the object type's database that gets used?

I think it's most useful for the _mutation's_ database to be used, because I would expect that one execution would happen against 1 database by default, because otherwise there are many race conditions and consistency issues that would come up under regular use. One of the issues is that some charms now rely on this behavior.. I personally think the update-status hook should've never been created, 5 minutes is so arbitrary, and polling for new status doesn't scale well, but there's not much the reactive framework can do at this point.

We've talked about this internally and our consensus is that the reactive framework actually shouldn't do anything when nothing has changed. Handlers should only be reinvoked if the flags they react to have changed. Re-invoking every handler whose flags match in every hook isn't a good idea.. So that's our long-ish-term plan: handlers will only get reinvoked when something has actually changed. What do you think of this?

Short-term plan is that you should simulate this behavior with flags. It is currently already possible to only run code when something has actually changed. I'm happy to take a look at your code and figure out how we can fix this with the current implementation. Are you free to share the code in question?

Regarding the composable conditions; I believe this is solving a different thing than dependencies. What this would do is eliminate the need for multiple predicate-functions such as `when` `when_not` and let the charm devs write it as a single statement.

If we would go with this idea but drop the dependencies we would still end up writing conditions like this:
```python
@condition('installed & db.available & configured & started')
def ready():
    ...
```

I do like the fact that it would make it more similar to a syntax that programmers are more used to write. But I also like the decorators in that it makes it very obvious what is going on using simple English terms. It's definitely a neat idea though!

A couple preliminary observations:

* All the rsid bits are basically noise. That's part of the revision marks mechanism Word has so can be safely ignored, including all the bits that appear in settings.xml.
* The bookmark _GoBack is just the way Word keeps track of where the cursor was when last editing, so that can go too.

*document.xml*

```bash
--- withStyle/word/document.xml

+++ withoutStyle/word/document.xml

@@ -19,15 +19,15 @@

     mc:Ignorable="w14 w15 wp14"
     >
   <w:body>
     <w:p>
       <w:r>
         <w:t xml:space="preserve">Now is the time for all </w:t>
       </w:r>
       <w:r>
         <w:rPr>
-          <w:rStyle w:val="highlightedChar"/>
+          <w:highlight w:val="yellow"/>
         </w:rPr>
         <w:t>good men</w:t>
       </w:r>
```

What this is telling us is that the character style was removed when you applied the highlight_color.

*styles.xml*
```bash
--- withStyle/word/styles.xml

+++ withoutStyle/word/styles.xml

@@ -426,23 +426,4 @@

     <w:semiHidden/>
     <w:unhideWhenUsed/>
   </w:style>
-  <w:style w:type="paragraph" w:customStyle="1" w:styleId="highlighted">
-    <w:name w:val="highlighted"/>
-    <w:basedOn w:val="Normal"/>
-    <w:link w:val="highlightedChar"/>
-    <w:qFormat/>
-    <w:rsid w:val="00666F4F"/>
-    <w:pPr>
-      <w:shd w:val="clear" w:color="auto" w:fill="FFFF00"/>
-    </w:pPr>
-  </w:style>
-  <w:style w:type="character" w:customStyle="1" w:styleId="highlightedChar">
-    <w:name w:val="highlighted Char"/>
-    <w:basedOn w:val="DefaultParagraphFont"/>
-    <w:link w:val="highlighted"/>
-    <w:rsid w:val="00666F4F"/>
-    <w:rPr>
-      <w:shd w:val="clear" w:color="auto" w:fill="FFFF00"/>
-    </w:rPr>
-  </w:style>
 </w:styles>
```

This part is inconclusive, just showing that two styles have been removed. I think what we need is a comparison of one that is created by python-docx and works in LibreOffice, and one that's created using the Word UI and works in Word. Just a clip of the style element itself will do the trick, something like this:

```xml
<w:style w:type="character" w:customStyle="1" w:styleId="highlightedChar">
  <w:name w:val="highlighted Char"/>
  <w:rPr>
    <w:shd w:val="clear" w:color="auto" w:fill="FFFF00"/>
  </w:rPr>
</w:style>
```

I'm thinking it's probably best to focus on the behavior of an independent character style for the moment, as it being linked to a paragraph style complicates things. It's definitely plausible that highlighting works in a character style, but not when that character style is linked to a paragraph style, perhaps because Microsoft thought it unlikely anyone would want to highlight a whole paragraph or something.

Well, just offhand, I'd say you'd need to account for the case when `w:cols` is not already present, if it's an optional element, which, of course, most of them are. To determine that, you'd need to have the relevant parts of the XML schema, which is part of what goes on the enhancement proposal, aka. "analysis page".

Then the element name is more elegantly handled by the internal function `qname()`, like `qname('w:num')`. That function lives in `docx.oxml` I believe.

Also, of course, it would need to become a method on section, or more likely a set of properties or perhaps an object of its own, depending on the scope of things that can eventually be done to columns, like perhaps set their width, margin, or space between.

So you can see there would be a lot of additional details to attend to before it could be merged, and of course the tests. Best to start off with the enhancement proposal page if you want to pursue it. There would be specified the API and all the required background collected. All of this affects the implementation so best to get that discussed and merged before going more of the implementation than you'd like to redo :)

The selenium 3 is an drop in replacement for selenium 2. The changes how to launch different Firefox versions are, as far I understand, not dependent on the selenium version. Example In my understanding FF 49.0.1 requires always the geckodriver setup, with selenium 2 and 3.

Therefore it should be possible fix the `Open Browser` keyword to support FF before and after geckodriver and without making changes to the keyword signature. In high level it could go something like this:
1) Try create driver with geckodriver set in the desired capabilities in the code side.
2) If Webdriver exception is raised, suppress it. Store error and try to create driver without geckodriver in the desired capabilities.
3) If error is raised, don't suppress it. Log also error from above, because it is impossible to know which error is correct.

The `Open Browser` keyword with remote url and desired capabilities needs some thinking. We could go with the same opinion as above and try automatically with and without geckodriver. Of course it needs some extra detection and logic has user set geckodriver in the desired capabilities. Or we could go with the option that it's on the user responsibility to set the desired capabilities correctly and we don't do anything in S2L side. I am thinking that the first way would be better from the regular user point of view and should not create too complex logic in the code side too.

The `Create Webdriver` keyword is  more complicated to solve, because of the different possibilities it offers. We could try something similar that with the `Open Browser` keyword, but I am afraid that the solution would do more harm than good. The other option is to do nothing and let the user define the desired capabilities correctly when using `Create Webdriver` keyword.

Any other thoughts, ideas, feedback how to solve the problem?

While I’m all for coverage measurements, it might be tricky here:

* C-accelerated builds take about 3x longer (2minuts becomes 6)
* Pure-python builds on CPython timeout after 10 minutes of no output; we’d have to increase verbosity to get around that (and then we might hit the log length limit). I don’t know how long they’ll take in actuality. 
* PyPy builds will probably do the same, only much worse (previous experience showed that PyPy under coverage is *really* slow. I want to say the zodb tests took about 20 minutes compared to 4, but maybe that’s better now)

Since the entire module is implemented in C, is coverage actually telling us much in that case? If yes, do we need to pay the cost in all environments or is one sufficient?

Is the extra build time worth it for the pure-python case? If we can find an appropriate verbosity I would guess yes, but only on CPython.

> Is the memory leak on the kernel side a concern? It doesn't seem like the widgets get closed automatically.

I think the point is that it isn't a memory leak if it's still communicating with something on the frontend. And the other point is that a weak reference to an active model won't do us any good because the comm still maintains a reference.

Another way to think about the lifecycle problem: the widget needs to be considered unreachable when *both* the frontend and the backend have no way of using it. That's a tricky problem.

> I'm wondering what about the for attribute? Isn't it enough for describing the input - label reference?

Yes, I think so. We would also need to check to see if that also enables the browser click-on-label-to-toggle-checkbox. If it's semantically equivalent (I think it is), then I wouldn't be opposed to a PR changing the DOM structure to have a label `for` attribute.

I'd like to point out a small bug, not sure how you suggest to fix it.

The learning rate (`lr`) is not among the model parameters that are saved in the `bin` file by `Args->save()` ([here](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/3e64bf0f5b916532b34be6706c161d7d0a4957a4/src/args.cc#L245)) and loaded back by `Args->load()` ([here](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/3e64bf0f5b916532b34be6706c161d7d0a4957a4/src/args.cc#L261)). 

Thus [this line](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/pull/423/files#diff-0c9c4a3d7c5d19e9fe56d939f0b5d94dR727) from your PR won't work as intended: if you don't specify `-lr` from the CLI, then the default is used (`0.05` from [here](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/3e64bf0f5b916532b34be6706c161d7d0a4957a4/src/args.cc#L20)), regardless of what you've been using in the previous iterations.

Do you want to fix it by patching `args.cc` to save/load `lr` in the `.bin` file?

To summarise:

for the following params, their values from the saved model override those from the CLI:
* `dim`
* `ws`
* `neg`
* `wordNgrams`
* `loss`
* `model`

while for the following parameters, their values from the CLI override those in the saved model:
* `epoch`
* `minCount`
* `bucket`
* `minn`
* `maxn`
* `lrUpdateRate`
* `t`
* `lr` (currently broken, see above)

I think me and @ralphiee22 talked about using better names for these roles like in the PR when implementing this, but weren't sure what the implications would be for backwards compatibility and syncing.

My comments are split into two parts:

Interface
---------

The interface as demonstrated in the tests. For a lot of Models (anything inheriting from `KolibriSyncableModel`) it's necessary to specify a `dataset` field. I understand the necessity of this -- it's a requirement for interfacing with Morango that can't be handled in a general way easily. But there *are* still several special cases that crop up where we *can* hide the details of `dataset` management. Requiring future devs to deal with `dataset`s unnecessarily creates an avoidable cognitive burden. We should aim to make using these Models as simple as possible -- if we can hide the details in many usual cases then we benefit by avoiding potential future errors (and a lot of typing).

I have the following suggestions to that end:

1. For `Collection`s its hard to generalize `dataset` management. But for the proxy models it's easy. There's a one-to-one correspondence between the `Facility` Model and `dataset`s -- so create a `dataset` implicitly when you create a `Facility`. For subcollections, if we require `parent` is non-null, then we can infer the dataset implicitly by finding the root `Facility`. We can hide `dataset` management by encouraging using the proxies, while still allowing flexibility by using the base `Collection`.

1. For `Role`s of all types, because they represent a many-to-many relationship with `Collection`s, we can infer the `dataset` through the `Collection`.

2. For `FacilityUser`s if we require a foreign-key to a `Facility`, then the dataset can again be inferred. Our code will read more naturally and be less coupled to Morango if we enforce having an associated `Facility` instead of requiring the `dataset` to be set. Compare the following interfaces:

```python
dataset = FacilityDataset.objects.create()
# It's an error to forget the dataset -- but that might be surprising!
# user = FacilityUser.objects.create(username="foo")
user = FacilityUser.objects.create(username="foo", dataset=dataset)

# How can I get to a user's facility?
# Through the roles? What if they don't have any roles?
user.role_set.filter(collection__kind=Collection.KIND_FACILITY)
# There's no way to get at it through the dataset either, which
# is a little counterintuitive -- FacilityDataset should be in 1-to-1
# correspondence with Facilities.
```

```python
facility = Facility.objects.create()
# If we require a non-null ForeignKey field, forgetting the facility is an error, too!
# This is de-coupled from Morango.
# Plus, it's in the name of the model.
user = FacilityUser.objects.create(username="foo", facility=facility)

# I can get a user's facility very simply!
user.facility
```

Implementation
--------------

**Collection proxy model methods vs. base Collection methods**:
I get the appeal of moving all the proxy model methods onto the base `Collection`. It makes it really easy to add *any* role type to *any* kind of `Collection`. It's nice to have that flexibility built in. But in this case I think having too much flexibility could lead to mistakes. We clearly identified what it means to be an "admin for a facility" (and coach->classroom, learner->learner group). It makes a certain sense to allow e.g. admin->classroom, even though we haven't really discussed the implications. But other cases are more ambiguous. For instance, what does it mean to have an "admin for a learner group" or a "student for a facility"? 

My suggestion is to move methods like `add_admin` and `add_coach` back onto the proxy models -- then it becomes explicit which relationships are canonical and which are ambiguous. Fully general `Role`s can still be created by using the `Collection.add_user` method, so no flexibility is lost, and if we encourage using the proxy models then we can catch simple mistakes.

**DeviceOwner and `_require_dataset` attribute**:
The only place where `_require_dataset` is set to `False` is for DeviceOwners. More specifically, it's problematic that `DeviceOwner` should inherit from `SyncableModel` at all when it's explicitly the intent that `DeviceOwners` are *never* synced. Handling this single case leads to extra complexity in `KolibriSyncableModel`. The solution is for `DeviceOwner` to *not* inherit from `KolibriSyncableModel` and remove the logic for handling that case. We had discussed having two separate non-abstract user Models -- I think this is a really compelling reason to pursue that. I was worried earlier that it might not play nicely with `django.contrib.auth`, but based on our previous discussions and the example here I think it's preferable.

I think if we really want to make the header clickable in addition to `view more` then we probably want to do it as a click listener, which is a little smelly. The alternative is not to have the heading clickable, and just leave it to `view more` which makes sense to me.

Less magic strings is good, so it seems a nice idea to replace `@validates('quantity')` with `@validates(quantity)`.

I would however like to point out that  schema-level validators may check how two or more fields interact with each other. A recently introduced feature allows skipping schema-level validation if the validation of the field itself failed, it can be generalized to multiple fields, too. There's not much sense in checking how two, say, integer parameters interact with each other if one of those is unexpectedly a dictionary. Current API could support that naturally with `@validates([quantity, quality, speed])`, but it is not that obvious to me how to do that with the proposed one.

Oh, right. So it's a test-specific form of retry.

All the pools are designed to be used via a Python context manager, which prevents any possibility of leaked sessions (that is the point of the context manager).  E.g.:

```python
with pool.session() as session:
    session.read(...)
    session.run_in_transaction(func)
```

No matter what, the session gets returned to the pool at the end of the `with` block.  In the case of `BurstyPool`,  if the pool is over its target size at that point, the session is just discarded (after a call to its `delete` API).

If we make `BurstyPool` have a hard cap on its size, then we might as well just delete it:  the `FixedSizePool` fills that niche already: when empty,  its `get()` blocks for a configurable timeout, and raises an exception if no session can be acquired.

My thought on this: we should change how we convert to/from the [Load/Query/Copy/Extract]JobConfig classes' API representation.

The current way is to return the `job.configuration.[load|query|copy|extract]` dictionary 

```
{ 'writeDispostion': 'WRITE_TRUNCATE', ... }
```

Currently the Job class add the [load|query|copy|extract] key (plus moves up shared options such as `dryRun` to the `configuration` dictionary. I propose we return the whole configuration dictionary.

```
{ 'load': { 'writeDispostion': 'WRITE_TRUNCATE', ... }}
```

Then the BigQuery backend can complain when it gets two job types in the same resource. Also, it will make it easier to implement new properties that are shared among all job configuration types such as 'labels'.

The clock skew daemon literally just runs

This shouldn't change the timezone nor should the system clock be exactly 1 hour off. If it is ever off, it will be behind and get synced forward.

Re: your original question on disabling the clock skew daemon. Yes, you [certainly can](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages#configuration). You can create a config file with the following contents to do so: `/etc/default/instance_configs.cfg.template`
```
[Daemons]
clock_skew_daemon = false
```

If you do this before the update- the clock skew daemon won't be started. The effects will be that when a live migration occurs, system clocks between VM's can be out of sync until NTP runs again. This matters mostly for database servers or any other set of servers that need to be in sync.

Okay, I found it.

I did consider making this a part of marker (a different kind of icon), but I didn't do it for two reasons.
- Leaflet doesn't actually include this as part of marker, it makes it a different kind of icon. But the folium API combines the marker and the icon into same call. Adding support for a different kind of icon in the marker API seemed like it would make it too complex - there are already 3 parameters dealing with icon (`marker-icon`, `clustered_marker` and `icon_angle`) and adding more would turn the API into something out of `matplotlib` :)
- I could have restructured this to more closely mirror the leaflet API - to have a `simple_icon` constructor which would be passed in to a `simple_marker`, but I wasn't sure how that would fit in with your architecture. You don't seem to do that anywhere else.
- It also seemed to me that the main use of the div icon (bunch of little boxes with not much decoration) would be if there are a lot of them in close proximity. So I optimized the API for that. Note that unlike `simple_marker`, it doesn't not take a single location, but instead, a list of locations and popups.

Hi Jeisk, I'm not sure what you're trying to do... You want a rectangle that if you click on it, another line appears? 

This is as far as I know not possible in folium. So this would probably require you to manipulate the underlying Leaflet Javascript. For example a click event on the rectangle polygon, that calls the AddTo method on the Polyline. I would recommend developing this in a [JSFiddle](https://jsfiddle.net/jsanz/Pe5xU/). Also, checkout the Leaflet documentation for how to do this:
http://leafletjs.com/reference-1.3.0.html#polyline

If you find some Javascript that works for you, you can add it to your map through folium:
```
m = folium.Map()
code = 'some javascript code'
m.get_root().script.add_child(folium.Element(code))
m
```

Also it would be nice if you can report your progress back here so others can learn from it as well!

import os

def test_classify(vr, data):
    with open(data, "rb") as f:
        try:
            r = vr.classify(images_file=f, classifier_ids="dogs_634305779")
            with open("responses.txt", "a") as responses:
                responses.write("{}: ALL GOOD\n".format(f.name))

            with open("responses.txt", "a") as responses:
                responses.write("{}: {}\n".format(f.name, e))


if __name__ == '__main__':

    import argparse
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("api_key")
    args = vars(ap.parse_args())

    vr = VisualRecognitionV3(api_key=args["api_key"], version='2016-05-20')

    for f in os.listdir(os.getcwd()):
        if f.split(".")[-1] in {"jpg", "zip"}:
            print("Testing on: {}".format(f))
            test_classify(vr, f)
